question: One general property common to most metals is that they

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a general property of metals.
explanation.principle: Metals contain mobile electrons that can carry electric charge.
steps: In metallic bonding, positive metal ions are surrounded by delocalized electrons.
These electrons can move through the structure.
Their movement allows electric current to pass.
Therefore most metals conduct electricity well.
explanation.simple_explanation: Metals conduct electricity because of their free-moving electrons. Check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: Metals are usually malleable because

options.A: their atoms are held by weak ionic forces
options.B: layers of atoms can slide without destroying metallic bonding completely

answer_text: layers of atoms can slide without destroying metallic bonding completely

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why metals can be hammered into sheets.
explanation.principle: Malleability depends on the nature of metallic bonding.
steps: Metals consist of positive ions in layers surrounded by delocalized electrons.
When force is applied, these layers can slide.
The metallic bond is not completely broken because the electron sea still holds the ions together.
Therefore metals are often malleable.
explanation.final_answer: B. layers of atoms can slide without destroying metallic bonding completely
explanation.simple_explanation: Metallic bonding helps explain malleability. Check our "Tutorial section" to learn more.

question: Metals conduct heat effectively mainly because they possess

options.B: mobile electrons that transfer energy quickly

answer_text: mobile electrons that transfer energy quickly

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why metals are good conductors of heat.
explanation.principle: The same delocalized electrons that conduct electricity also help carry thermal energy.
steps: When one part of a metal is heated, electrons gain kinetic energy.
They move and transfer energy rapidly through the structure.
This spreads heat quickly across the metal.
Therefore metals conduct heat effectively because they possess mobile electrons.
explanation.final_answer: B. mobile electrons that transfer energy quickly
explanation.simple_explanation: Free electrons help metals conduct both heat and electricity. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallic bonding.

question: Metals are described as ductile because they can be

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the meaning of ductility.
explanation.principle: Ductility refers to the ability of a substance to be drawn into wires.
steps: Metals can often be stretched without breaking quickly.
That is why many electrical wires are made of metals such as copper.
This property is called ductility.
Therefore ductile means can be drawn into wires.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ductility means a metal can be drawn into wires. Check our "Tutorial section" for metal properties.

question: The shiny appearance of a freshly cut metal surface is referred to as

explanation.question_restate: We are to name the shiny appearance shown by many metals.
explanation.principle: Many metals reflect light strongly from their surfaces.
steps: A newly cut metallic surface often appears bright and shiny.
This visual property is called metallic lustre.
It is one of the common physical properties of metals.
Therefore the shiny appearance is referred to as lustre.
explanation.simple_explanation: Lustre is the shine seen on many metallic surfaces. Check our "Tutorial section" for common physical properties.

question: A chemical property common to many metals is that they

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a typical chemical behavior of metals.
explanation.principle: Metals usually become positive ions by losing electrons.
steps: Metals tend to have relatively low ionization energies compared with many non-metals.
They therefore lose electrons more readily in chemical reactions.
This produces positive ions known as cations.
So a common chemical property is that they lose electrons to form cations.
explanation.final_answer: B. lose electrons to form cations
explanation.simple_explanation: Metals typically form positive ions by losing electrons. Check our "Tutorial section" for oxidation and ions.

question: Why are many metals sonorous?

options.B: their rigid metallic structures allow vibrations to pass through them

answer_text: their rigid metallic structures allow vibrations to pass through them

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why metals produce a ringing sound when struck.
explanation.principle: Sonority depends on how vibrations travel through a material.
steps: When a metal is struck, its particles vibrate.
These vibrations pass effectively through the solid metallic structure.
This produces the familiar ringing sound.
Therefore many metals are sonorous because their structures allow vibrations to pass through them.
explanation.final_answer: B. their rigid metallic structures allow vibrations to pass through them
explanation.simple_explanation: Sonority is the ringing sound many metals make when struck. Check our "Tutorial section" for metal properties.

question: Which of the following is NOT a general property of metals?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify what is not generally true of metals.
explanation.principle: Most metals are not usually described as universally brittle.
steps: Many metals are malleable and ductile.
They also commonly show lustre and good conductivity.
Although a few metals may be brittle under some conditions, brittleness is not a universal metallic property.
Therefore brittleness as a universal feature is not a general property of metals.
explanation.final_answer: C. brittleness as a universal feature
explanation.simple_explanation: Not every solid property applies to all metals; focus on the common ones. Check our "Tutorial section" for summaries.

question: The ability of metals to form alloys is best explained by the fact that

options.A: their atoms can mix within metallic lattices

answer_text: their atoms can mix within metallic lattices

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why different metals can be combined to form alloys.
explanation.principle: An alloy is a mixture involving metals, often with atoms sharing a metallic structure.
steps: Metal atoms can fit into or replace positions in a metallic lattice.
This allows different metals to mix on the atomic scale.
The resulting substance often has improved properties.
Therefore metals can form alloys because their atoms can mix within metallic lattices.
explanation.final_answer: A. their atoms can mix within metallic lattices
explanation.simple_explanation: Alloys form because different atoms can share a metallic structure. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloys.

question: Metals are often used for cooking pots mainly because they are

explanation.question_restate: We are to link a property of metals with a practical use.
explanation.principle: Cooking pots need materials that can transfer heat efficiently.
steps: Heat must move from the stove into the food quickly.
Metals allow heat to spread through the pot effectively.
That is why many pots are made from metals or metal alloys.
Therefore metals are used mainly because they are good conductors of heat.
explanation.simple_explanation: Uses of metals often follow directly from their properties. Check our "Tutorial section" for more applied examples.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium hydroxide / electrolysis of brine
question: Sodium hydroxide is produced industrially mainly by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the industrial method for producing sodium hydroxide.
explanation.principle: Sodium hydroxide is one of the products of the chlor-alkali process.
steps: Brine is concentrated sodium chloride solution.
When brine is electrolyzed, chlorine is liberated at the anode and hydrogen at the cathode.
The remaining solution becomes rich in sodium hydroxide.
Therefore sodium hydroxide is produced mainly by electrolysis of brine.
explanation.simple_explanation: NaOH is a major product of brine electrolysis. Check our "Tutorial section" for the chlor-alkali process.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium hydroxide / electrolysis of brine
question: During electrolysis of concentrated sodium chloride solution, chlorine is evolved at the

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the electrode at which chlorine is formed during brine electrolysis.
explanation.principle: Oxidation occurs at the anode.
steps: Chloride ions move to the positive electrode.
There they lose electrons to form chlorine gas.
The anode reaction is 2Cl⁻ → Cl₂ + 2e⁻.
Therefore chlorine is evolved at the anode.
explanation.simple_explanation: Chloride ions are oxidized at the anode to produce chlorine. Check our "Tutorial section" for electrode products.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) / Solvay process
question: The industrial process used to manufacture sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) is the

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the process used in making sodium trioxocarbonate(IV).
explanation.principle: Sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) is another name for sodium carbonate, Na₂CO₃.
steps: The Solvay process is the standard industrial method for producing sodium carbonate.
It makes use of brine, ammonia and carbon(IV) oxide.
This process is important in chemical industry.
Therefore the correct answer is Solvay process.
explanation.simple_explanation: The Solvay process is the major industrial route to sodium carbonate. Check our "Tutorial section" for process names.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium trioxocarbonate(IV)
question: Sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) is commonly known as

explanation.question_restate: We are to match the compound with its common name.
explanation.principle: Sodium trioxocarbonate(IV), Na₂CO₃, is commonly called washing soda.
steps: The systematic name refers to sodium carbonate.
Its common practical name is washing soda.
It is used in laundry and glass manufacture.
Therefore sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) is washing soda.
explanation.simple_explanation: It helps to know both systematic and common names for exam questions. Check our "Tutorial section" for naming practice.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium hydrogen trioxocarbonate(IV)
question: Sodium hydrogen trioxocarbonate(IV) is best represented by the formula

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct formula for sodium hydrogen trioxocarbonate(IV).
explanation.principle: This compound is sodium hydrogencarbonate, commonly called sodium bicarbonate.
steps: The hydrogen trioxocarbonate(IV) ion is HCO₃⁻.
Combining this with Na⁺ gives NaHCO₃.
Na₂CO₃ is sodium trioxocarbonate(IV), not the hydrogen salt.
Therefore the correct formula is NaHCO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogencarbonates contain HCO₃⁻, not CO₃²⁻. Check our "Tutorial section" for carbonate naming.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium chloride / economic importance of sea water
question: Sea water is economically important because it is a major source of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an important economic product obtained from sea water.
explanation.principle: Sea water contains dissolved salts, especially sodium chloride.
steps: Large quantities of salt can be recovered from sea water.
This salt has many domestic and industrial uses.
Sea water also has broader economic value because of other dissolved substances.
Therefore one major reason is that it is a major source of sodium chloride.
explanation.simple_explanation: Sea water is economically important partly because it provides common salt. Check our "Tutorial section" for applied chemistry.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium chloride
question: The common formula of sodium chloride is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the formula for sodium chloride.
explanation.principle: Sodium chloride is made of Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions in a 1:1 ratio.
steps: Sodium forms Na⁺ by losing one electron.
Chlorine forms Cl⁻ by gaining one electron.
A neutral compound therefore needs one of each ion.
So the formula is NaCl.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ionic formulas come from balancing charges. Check our "Tutorial section" for formula writing.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium hydroxide
question: A common name for sodium hydroxide is

explanation.question_restate: We are to match sodium hydroxide with its common name.
explanation.principle: NaOH is commonly called caustic soda.
steps: Sodium hydroxide has the formula NaOH.
It is a strong alkali with many industrial uses.
Its widely used common name is caustic soda.
Therefore the correct answer is caustic soda.
explanation.simple_explanation: Both common and systematic names are useful for JAMB-style questions. Check our "Tutorial section" for naming practice.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: Solvay process / sodium trioxocarbonate(IV)
question: One important use of sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) is in the manufacture of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an important industrial use of sodium trioxocarbonate(IV).
explanation.principle: Sodium carbonate is an important raw material in glass making.
steps: Na₂CO₃ helps lower the melting point of silica in glass manufacture.
This makes glass production easier and more economical.
That is one reason sodium carbonate is industrially important.
Therefore it is used in the manufacture of glass.
explanation.simple_explanation: Sodium carbonate is important in the glass industry. Check our "Tutorial section" for uses of sodium compounds.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: economic importance of sea water
question: Besides sodium chloride, sea water is economically important because it also provides

options.A: other dissolved salts and useful minerals

answer_text: other dissolved salts and useful minerals

explanation.question_restate: We are to state a broader reason why sea water is economically important.
explanation.principle: Sea water contains many dissolved substances besides common salt.
steps: These include other useful salts and mineral resources.
Such substances can be recovered for industrial and domestic use.
This makes sea water an economically valuable natural resource.
Therefore it also provides other dissolved salts and useful minerals.
explanation.final_answer: A. other dissolved salts and useful minerals
explanation.simple_explanation: Sea water is valuable because it contains more than just common salt. Check our "Tutorial section" for applied chemistry.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium oxide / sea shells
question: Calcium oxide is prepared by strongly heating sea shells which are mainly

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main substance in sea shells used to prepare calcium oxide.
explanation.principle: Sea shells are largely made of calcium trioxocarbonate(IV), CaCO₃.
steps: When CaCO₃ is strongly heated, it decomposes.
The products are calcium oxide and carbon(IV) oxide.
This is called thermal decomposition or calcination.
Therefore sea shells are mainly CaCO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Sea shells provide CaCO₃, which is heated to make CaO. Check our "Tutorial section" for calcium compounds.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium oxide
question: The thermal decomposition of calcium trioxocarbonate(IV) is represented by

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the correct equation for preparing calcium oxide from calcium trioxocarbonate(IV).
explanation.principle: On strong heating, carbonates of many metals decompose into the oxide and carbon(IV) oxide.
steps: Calcium trioxocarbonate(IV) is CaCO₃.
When heated strongly, it breaks down into calcium oxide and CO₂.
This is a decomposition reaction.
Therefore the correct equation is CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Thermal decomposition of CaCO₃ is a standard exam equation. Check our "Tutorial section" for more examples.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium hydroxide
question: Calcium hydroxide is commonly known as

explanation.question_restate: We are to match the compound Ca(OH)₂ with its common name.
explanation.principle: Calcium hydroxide is commonly called slaked lime.
steps: Quicklime is calcium oxide, CaO.
When water is added to CaO, slaked lime, Ca(OH)₂, is formed.
Thus calcium hydroxide is called slaked lime.
Therefore the correct answer is slaked lime.
explanation.simple_explanation: Know the difference between quicklime and slaked lime. Check our "Tutorial section" for calcium compounds.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium hydroxide
question: Limewater is

explanation.question_restate: We are to define limewater.
explanation.principle: Limewater is the clear aqueous solution of calcium hydroxide.
steps: When a small amount of Ca(OH)₂ dissolves in water, the solution formed is limewater.
It is commonly used to test for carbon(IV) oxide.
It is not molten calcium chloride or solid calcium carbonate.
Therefore limewater is a solution of Ca(OH)₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Limewater is the clear solution of slaked lime in water. Check our "Tutorial section" for common lab reagents.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium trioxocarbonate(IV)
question: When carbon(IV) oxide is passed through limewater, the milkiness is due to formation of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the white precipitate formed in the limewater test.
explanation.principle: Carbon(IV) oxide reacts with calcium hydroxide to produce insoluble calcium trioxocarbonate(IV).
steps: Limewater is Ca(OH)₂ solution.
CO₂ reacts with it to form CaCO₃ and water.
CaCO₃ is white and insoluble.
Therefore the milkiness is due to CaCO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: The limewater test gives calcium carbonate as a white precipitate. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: cement composition
question: A major constituent of cement is

options.A: calcium compounds such as lime and silicates

answer_text: calcium compounds such as lime and silicates

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the general composition of cement.
explanation.principle: Cement is made largely from calcium-containing materials and silicates.
steps: Raw materials for cement include limestone and clay.
These provide calcium compounds and silicates.
The final material contains important calcium silicates and related substances.
Therefore a major constituent of cement is calcium compounds such as lime and silicates.
explanation.final_answer: A. calcium compounds such as lime and silicates
explanation.simple_explanation: Cement chemistry is strongly based on calcium compounds. Check our "Tutorial section" for building materials.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: setting of mortar
question: Mortar sets mainly because slaked lime reacts with

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why mortar hardens on standing.
explanation.principle: Mortar setting involves conversion of calcium hydroxide back to calcium carbonate.
steps: Slaked lime in mortar is Ca(OH)₂.
It reacts gradually with CO₂ from the air.
This forms hard CaCO₃ and water.
Therefore mortar sets because slaked lime reacts with carbon(IV) oxide in air.
explanation.simple_explanation: Mortar hardens as Ca(OH)₂ changes back to CaCO₃. Check our "Tutorial section" for construction chemistry.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: setting of mortar
question: The reaction important in setting of mortar is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the equation that explains mortar setting.
explanation.principle: The hardening comes from formation of calcium carbonate from calcium hydroxide.
steps: Mortar contains slaked lime, Ca(OH)₂.
Over time it reacts with carbon(IV) oxide from the air.
The product CaCO₃ is hard and helps bind the material.
Therefore the key equation is Ca(OH)₂ + CO₂ → CaCO₃ + H₂O.
explanation.simple_explanation: This equation shows how mortar hardens on standing. Check our "Tutorial section" for building materials.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: test for Ca²⁺
question: A common confirmatory test for Ca²⁺ in school chemistry is the formation of a brick-red colour in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common test for calcium ions.
explanation.principle: Some metal ions give characteristic flame colours.
steps: Calcium ions give a brick-red or orange-red flame coloration.
This is useful in simple qualitative analysis.
It helps distinguish calcium from sodium and potassium.
Therefore a common confirmatory test is the flame test.
explanation.simple_explanation: Calcium gives a brick-red flame. Check our "Tutorial section" for flame tests.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium oxide / calcium hydroxide
question: Which sequence correctly shows conversion from calcium trioxocarbonate(IV) to slaked lime?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct sequence from limestone or sea shells to slaked lime.
explanation.principle: First calcium carbonate is heated to give calcium oxide, then water is added to make calcium hydroxide.
steps: CaCO₃ on heating gives CaO and CO₂.
Calcium oxide is quicklime.
Adding water to quicklime gives Ca(OH)₂, called slaked lime.
Therefore the correct sequence is CaCO₃ → CaO → Ca(OH)₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: This is an important sequence for understanding lime chemistry. Check our "Tutorial section" for the full cycle.

question: The chief ore of aluminium is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main ore from which aluminium is obtained.
explanation.principle: Bauxite is the principal commercial ore of aluminium.
steps: Bauxite contains hydrated aluminium oxides and related impurities.
It is purified before aluminium extraction.
This makes it the chief ore of aluminium.
Therefore the correct answer is bauxite.
explanation.simple_explanation: Bauxite is the major ore used for aluminium production. Check our "Tutorial section" for extraction topics.

question: Before aluminium is extracted electrolytically, bauxite is purified mainly to remove

options.A: impurities such as iron oxides and silica

answer_text: impurities such as iron oxides and silica

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why bauxite is purified before extraction.
explanation.principle: Purification improves efficiency by removing unwanted materials from the ore.
steps: Natural bauxite contains impurities including iron compounds and silica.
These impurities would interfere with efficient extraction.
So purification is carried out before electrolysis.
Therefore the main purpose is to remove impurities such as iron oxides and silica.
explanation.final_answer: A. impurities such as iron oxides and silica
explanation.simple_explanation: Ore purification helps make extraction more efficient. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy basics.

question: Aluminium is extracted from purified alumina mainly by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the method used to obtain aluminium from alumina.
explanation.principle: Aluminium is too reactive to be extracted by simple carbon reduction.
steps: The oxide of aluminium is very stable.
So electrolysis is used instead of reduction by carbon.
Molten or dissolved alumina in a suitable medium is electrolyzed.
Therefore aluminium is extracted mainly by electrolysis.
explanation.simple_explanation: Highly reactive metals such as aluminium are extracted by electrolysis. Check our "Tutorial section" for extraction methods.

question: Why is electrolysis used for aluminium extraction rather than carbon reduction?

options.A: aluminium oxide is very stable and aluminium is highly reactive

answer_text: aluminium oxide is very stable and aluminium is highly reactive

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why carbon reduction is not the normal method for extracting aluminium.
explanation.principle: Very reactive metals usually require electrolysis because their compounds are too stable for easy reduction by carbon.
steps: Aluminium lies high in the reactivity series.
Its oxide is very stable.
Carbon cannot reduce it economically in the usual way.
Therefore electrolysis is used because aluminium oxide is very stable and aluminium is highly reactive.
explanation.final_answer: A. aluminium oxide is very stable and aluminium is highly reactive
explanation.simple_explanation: Use the reactivity series to predict extraction methods. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: One important property of aluminium that makes it useful for cooking utensils is that it

options.A: is a poor conductor of heat
options.B: conducts heat well and resists corrosion

answer_text: conducts heat well and resists corrosion

explanation.question_restate: We are to connect a property of aluminium with one of its uses.
explanation.principle: Useful utensils should transfer heat well and not corrode easily.
steps: Aluminium is a good conductor of heat.
It is also protected by a thin oxide layer that helps resist corrosion.
These properties make it suitable for many household utensils.
Therefore the correct answer is that it conducts heat well and resists corrosion.
explanation.final_answer: B. conducts heat well and resists corrosion
explanation.simple_explanation: Uses of aluminium are tied closely to its properties. Check our "Tutorial section" for applications.

question: Aluminium resists corrosion largely because it forms

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why aluminium does not corrode rapidly like some other metals.
explanation.principle: Its surface becomes covered with a thin protective layer.
steps: When fresh aluminium is exposed to air, it reacts with oxygen.
A thin layer of aluminium oxide forms on the surface.
This layer protects the metal underneath from further attack.
Therefore aluminium resists corrosion because it forms a protective oxide film.
explanation.simple_explanation: The oxide film protects aluminium from further corrosion. Check our "Tutorial section" for corrosion resistance.

question: A common use of aluminium is in aircraft manufacture mainly because it is

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why aluminium is used in aircraft manufacture.
explanation.principle: Aircraft materials should be as light as possible while remaining strong.
steps: Aluminium has relatively low density.
When made into suitable alloys, it becomes stronger.
This gives a high strength-to-weight advantage.
Therefore it is used mainly because it is light and strong when alloyed.
explanation.final_answer: B. light and strong when alloyed
explanation.simple_explanation: Low density is a major reason aluminium and its alloys are important in transport. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloys and uses.

question: A common qualitative behavior of Al³⁺ with aqueous NaOH is that it forms a white precipitate which

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a characteristic test behavior of aluminium ions with sodium hydroxide.
explanation.principle: Aluminium hydroxide is amphoteric.
steps: When NaOH is added to Al³⁺ solution, a white precipitate of aluminium hydroxide forms.
Because aluminium hydroxide is amphoteric, it dissolves in excess NaOH.
This behavior helps distinguish Al³⁺ from some other ions.
Therefore the white precipitate dissolves in excess NaOH.
explanation.simple_explanation: The amphoteric nature of aluminium hydroxide is important in qualitative analysis. Check our "Tutorial section" for ion tests.

question: The precipitate formed when a little NaOH is added to a solution containing Al³⁺ is usually

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the colour of the precipitate formed with Al³⁺ and NaOH.
explanation.principle: Aluminium hydroxide is a white gelatinous precipitate.
steps: Adding a small amount of NaOH to Al³⁺ produces Al(OH)₃.
This hydroxide appears white.
In excess NaOH it dissolves because of amphoterism.
Therefore the initial precipitate is white.
explanation.simple_explanation: Aluminium hydroxide appears as a white precipitate. Check our "Tutorial section" for qualitative analysis.

question: The substance actually electrolyzed for aluminium extraction is mainly purified

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the purified aluminium compound used in extraction.
explanation.principle: Purified bauxite is converted into alumina before electrolysis.
steps: Bauxite is first purified.
The useful aluminium compound obtained is alumina, Al₂O₃.
This is the substance used in the electrolytic process.
Therefore the material electrolyzed is mainly purified alumina.
explanation.simple_explanation: Alumina is the purified aluminium oxide used in extraction. Check our "Tutorial section" for extraction stages.

question: The chief ore of tin is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main ore of tin.
explanation.principle: Cassiterite is the principal ore from which tin is extracted.
steps: Cassiterite is mainly tin(IV) oxide, SnO₂.
It is the common commercial source of tin.
This makes it important in metallurgy.
Therefore the chief ore of tin is cassiterite.
explanation.simple_explanation: Cassiterite is the major ore used for tin extraction. Check our "Tutorial section" for ore names.

question: Tin is commonly extracted from cassiterite by

options.B: reduction of the oxide with carbon

answer_text: reduction of the oxide with carbon

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the method used to obtain tin from cassiterite.
explanation.principle: Cassiterite is tin(IV) oxide, which can be reduced by carbon in furnace processes.
steps: Tin is less reactive than aluminium, sodium and calcium.
Its oxide can therefore be reduced by carbon.
This makes carbon reduction a suitable extraction method.
Therefore tin is extracted by reduction of the oxide with carbon.
explanation.final_answer: B. reduction of the oxide with carbon
explanation.simple_explanation: Less reactive metals can often be extracted by carbon reduction. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: One property that makes tin useful for coating steel food cans is that tin

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why tin is used for coating steel cans.
explanation.principle: A protective coating should resist corrosion.
steps: Tin is less reactive than iron in many ordinary conditions.
A tin coating protects the steel beneath from direct contact with air and moisture.
This helps reduce rusting.
Therefore tin is used because it resists corrosion better than iron.
explanation.final_answer: B. resists corrosion better than iron
explanation.simple_explanation: Tin-plating protects steel largely because tin is more corrosion-resistant. Check our "Tutorial section" for uses of metals.

question: Tin is often used in soft solder because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain one reason tin is used in solder.
explanation.principle: Solder materials should melt at relatively low temperatures to join metals easily.
steps: Tin can alloy with lead and some other metals.
These alloys can have lower melting points than many structural metals.
That makes them useful in joining electrical or metal parts.
Therefore tin is used because it forms useful low-melting alloys.
explanation.simple_explanation: Properties of alloys often explain their practical uses. Check our "Tutorial section" for solder and alloys.

question: Cassiterite is mainly

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the formula of cassiterite.
explanation.principle: Cassiterite is tin(IV) oxide.
steps: The ore is a natural oxide of tin.
Tin is present mainly in the +4 oxidation state in cassiterite.
So its formula is SnO₂.
Therefore the correct answer is SnO₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Know both ore names and their formulas for exam questions. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: A common use of tin is in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common use of tin.
explanation.principle: Tin is often used as a protective coating for other metals such as steel.
steps: Tin plating helps protect the surface of steel from corrosion.
This is especially useful in making cans and containers.
The practice is called tin-plating.
Therefore a common use of tin is tin-plating.
explanation.simple_explanation: Tin is widely known for its use in protective coatings. Check our "Tutorial section" for uses of metals.

question: Why can tin be extracted from its oxide by carbon reduction while aluminium cannot?

options.A: tin is lower than aluminium in the reactivity series
options.C: carbon is more reactive than all metals

answer_text: tin is lower than aluminium in the reactivity series

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare the extraction methods of tin and aluminium.
explanation.principle: Extraction methods depend strongly on reactivity.
steps: Tin is less reactive than aluminium.
Carbon can reduce tin(IV) oxide under furnace conditions.
Aluminium oxide is too stable and aluminium is too reactive for ordinary carbon reduction.
Therefore tin can be extracted this way because it is lower than aluminium in the reactivity series.
explanation.final_answer: A. tin is lower than aluminium in the reactivity series
explanation.simple_explanation: Reactivity helps explain why some metals use carbon reduction and others use electrolysis. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: Which statement about tin is correct?

options.A: it is commonly obtained from cassiterite

answer_text: it is commonly obtained from cassiterite

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a correct statement about tin.
explanation.principle: Cassiterite is the principal ore from which tin is extracted.
steps: Bauxite is associated with aluminium, not tin.
Tin is a metal and can form useful alloys.
Its main ore is cassiterite.
Therefore the correct statement is that it is commonly obtained from cassiterite.
explanation.final_answer: A. it is commonly obtained from cassiterite
explanation.simple_explanation: Ore names are common in JAMB-style questions. Check our "Tutorial section" for extraction summaries.

question: Tin plating is useful mainly because it

options.B: provides a protective coating against corrosion

answer_text: provides a protective coating against corrosion

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the purpose of coating steel with tin.
explanation.principle: A protective metal coating can reduce contact of steel with air and moisture.
steps: The tin layer covers the steel surface.
This reduces direct exposure of the steel to corrosive conditions.
As a result, rusting is reduced.
Therefore tin plating provides a protective coating against corrosion.
explanation.final_answer: B. provides a protective coating against corrosion
explanation.simple_explanation: Coatings are often used to control corrosion. Check our "Tutorial section" for applied metal chemistry.

question: Which pair is correctly matched?

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the correctly matched ore and metal.
explanation.principle: Cassiterite is the principal ore of tin.
steps: Bauxite is associated with aluminium.
Haematite is an iron ore.
Chalcopyrite is associated with copper.
Therefore cassiterite — tin ore is the correct match.
explanation.simple_explanation: Correct ore–metal matching is a standard exam skill. Check our "Tutorial section" for ore names.

question: A characteristic feature of many first transition series elements is that they have

options.A: completely filled d-subshells in all common states
options.B: partially filled d-orbitals in atoms or ions

answer_text: partially filled d-orbitals in atoms or ions

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a key characteristic of first transition series elements.
explanation.principle: Transition metals are associated with partly filled d-subshells.
steps: This incomplete d-subshell helps explain many transition-metal properties.
These include colored ions, variable oxidation states and complex formation.
So the important feature is partial filling of d-orbitals in atoms or ions.
Therefore the correct answer is partially filled d-orbitals in atoms or ions.
explanation.final_answer: B. partially filled d-orbitals in atoms or ions
explanation.simple_explanation: Partly filled d-orbitals help explain many transition-metal properties. Check our "Tutorial section" for transition metals.

question: A common property of first transition series metals is that they show

options.A: only one oxidation state in all compounds

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a characteristic chemical behavior of transition metals.
explanation.principle: Transition metals often use different numbers of electrons in bonding.
steps: Because the energies of the s and d electrons are relatively close, different numbers can be involved in bonding.
This leads to different oxidation states in compounds.
For example, iron commonly shows +2 and +3.
Therefore a common property is variable oxidation states.
explanation.simple_explanation: Variable oxidation states are a hallmark of many transition metals. Check our "Tutorial section" for examples.

subtopic: First transition series: complex ion formation
question: A complex ion is formed when a metal ion combines with

options.B: molecules or ions that donate electron pairs

answer_text: molecules or ions that donate electron pairs

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain what leads to the formation of a complex ion.
explanation.principle: Complex ions form when ligands donate lone pairs to a central metal ion.
steps: Ligands may be molecules such as NH₃ or ions such as Cl⁻.
These donate electron pairs to the metal ion.
The metal–ligand combination forms a complex ion.
Therefore a complex ion is formed when a metal ion combines with molecules or ions that donate electron pairs.
explanation.final_answer: B. molecules or ions that donate electron pairs
explanation.simple_explanation: Complex ions involve metal ions and electron-pair-donating ligands. Check our "Tutorial section" for coordination chemistry basics.

question: Many transition-metal ions are coloured mainly because of

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why many transition-metal ions show colour.
explanation.principle: The partly filled d-orbitals play a central role in the absorption of visible light.
steps: When light passes through a transition-metal solution, some wavelengths can be absorbed.
Electrons may move between split d-energy levels.
The remaining light gives the solution its observed color.
Therefore many transition-metal ions are colored mainly because of d-electron transitions.
explanation.simple_explanation: Colour in many transition-metal ions is tied to their d-electrons. Check our "Tutorial section" for transition-metal properties.

question: A common reason transition metals are good catalysts is that they

options.B: can use variable oxidation states during reaction pathways

answer_text: can use variable oxidation states during reaction pathways

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain one reason transition metals often act as effective catalysts.
explanation.principle: Catalysis by transition metals is often linked to their flexible electron arrangements.
steps: Transition metals can change oxidation state during reactions.
This can provide alternative pathways with lower activation energies.
They may also adsorb reactants on their surfaces.
Therefore they are good catalysts because they can use variable oxidation states during reaction pathways.
explanation.final_answer: B. can use variable oxidation states during reaction pathways
explanation.simple_explanation: Variable oxidation states help make many transition metals good catalysts. Check our "Tutorial section" for catalysis.

question: The first transition series begins with

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the element that starts the first transition series.
explanation.principle: The first transition series runs across the period containing scandium to zinc in school treatment.
steps: Scandium is the first element in this d-block row.
The series then continues across elements such as titanium, vanadium, chromium and iron.
This row is commonly called the first transition series.
Therefore it begins with scandium.
explanation.simple_explanation: The first transition series begins with scandium in school chemistry treatment. Check our "Tutorial section" for periodic-table sections.

question: Iron showing both Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ in compounds is an example of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the concept illustrated by Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺.
explanation.principle: When one element forms compounds in more than one oxidation state, it shows variable oxidation states.
steps: Iron can lose two electrons to form Fe²⁺.
It can also lose three electrons to form Fe³⁺.
This is characteristic of many transition metals.
Therefore the concept illustrated is variable oxidation states.
explanation.simple_explanation: Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ are classic examples of variable oxidation states. Check our "Tutorial section" for examples.

subtopic: First transition series: complex ion formation
question: A transition-metal complex ion usually contains a central metal ion surrounded by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify what surrounds the central metal ion in a complex.
explanation.principle: Ligands are the groups or molecules attached to the central metal ion.
steps: These ligands donate electron pairs to the metal ion.
They may be neutral molecules or negative ions.
Together they form a complex ion or complex compound.
Therefore the central metal ion is surrounded by ligands.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ligands are central to complex-ion formation. Check our "Tutorial section" for coordination chemistry basics.

question: A likely reason many ions of sodium are colourless while many transition-metal ions are coloured is that sodium ions

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare sodium ions with transition-metal ions in terms of colour.
explanation.principle: Colour in many transition-metal ions is linked to partly filled d-orbitals.
steps: Sodium ions do not have the partly filled d-subshell needed for the usual d–d transitions.
Many transition-metal ions do have such d-electron arrangements.
As a result, transition-metal ions are often colored while sodium ions are not.
Therefore sodium ions are colourless mainly because they have no partly filled d-subshell.
explanation.final_answer: A. have no partly filled d-subshell
explanation.simple_explanation: Partly filled d-subshells help explain the colors of transition-metal ions. Check our "Tutorial section" for comparisons.

question: Which statement best describes a catalyst?

options.B: it speeds a reaction without being used up overall
options.C: it always changes the products formed completely

answer_text: it speeds a reaction without being used up overall

explanation.question_restate: We are to define a catalyst correctly.
explanation.principle: A catalyst changes the rate of reaction by providing an easier pathway.
steps: It lowers the effective activation energy for the reaction.
It is regenerated by the end of the reaction mechanism overall.
So it is not consumed overall.
Therefore a catalyst speeds a reaction without being used up overall.
explanation.final_answer: B. it speeds a reaction without being used up overall
explanation.simple_explanation: Catalysts speed reactions without being consumed overall. Check our "Tutorial section" for catalysis basics.

subtopic: Iron: extraction from sulphide and oxide ores
question: A common oxide ore of iron is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common oxide ore of iron.
explanation.principle: Haematite is an important ore containing iron(III) oxide.
steps: Haematite is largely Fe₂O₃.
It is one of the chief ores used for iron extraction.
This makes it a standard example of an iron oxide ore.
Therefore the correct answer is haematite.
explanation.simple_explanation: Haematite is a major iron ore. Check our "Tutorial section" for ore names.

subtopic: Iron: extraction from sulphide and oxide ores
question: In the blast furnace, carbon(II) oxide helps in extraction of iron by

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the role of carbon(II) oxide in iron extraction.
explanation.principle: A reducing agent removes oxygen from a metal oxide.
steps: Iron ores such as Fe₂O₃ contain oxygen combined with iron.
CO reacts with the oxide and removes the oxygen.
This leaves iron metal behind and forms CO₂.
Therefore CO helps by removing oxygen from iron oxide.
explanation.final_answer: A. removing oxygen from iron oxide
explanation.simple_explanation: CO is the key reducing agent in the blast furnace. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: A common use of iron is in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a major use of iron.
explanation.principle: Iron is strong and widely available, making it useful in structural applications.
steps: Iron and steel are used for buildings, bridges and machinery.
Their strength makes them important engineering materials.
This is one of the biggest uses of iron-based materials.
Therefore a common use is in construction and engineering works.
explanation.simple_explanation: Iron is central to many engineering and structural uses. Check our "Tutorial section" for uses of metals.

subtopic: Iron: forms of iron / steel versus iron
question: Steel differs from pure iron mainly because steel is

options.A: an alloy of iron with small amounts of carbon and other elements

answer_text: an alloy of iron with small amounts of carbon and other elements

explanation.question_restate: We are to distinguish steel from pure iron.
explanation.principle: Steel is an alloy based on iron rather than pure iron alone.
steps: Pure iron contains mainly iron atoms.
Steel contains iron together with carbon and sometimes other elements.
This changes strength, hardness and other properties.
Therefore steel is an alloy of iron with small amounts of carbon and other elements.
explanation.final_answer: A. an alloy of iron with small amounts of carbon and other elements
explanation.simple_explanation: The difference between iron and steel is very important in materials chemistry. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloys.

question: Which pair correctly represents common ionic forms of iron?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the common ionic forms of iron in compounds.
explanation.principle: Iron commonly shows two oxidation states in school chemistry.
steps: Iron can form Fe²⁺ ions when it loses two electrons.
It can also form Fe³⁺ ions when it loses three electrons.
These two forms are widely tested in qualitative analysis.
Therefore the correct pair is Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺.
explanation.simple_explanation: Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ are the common ionic forms of iron in school chemistry. Check our "Tutorial section" for ion tests.

subtopic: Iron: tests for Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺
question: A solution containing Fe²⁺ gives with aqueous NaOH a precipitate that is usually

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the colour of the hydroxide precipitate formed from Fe²⁺ with sodium hydroxide.
explanation.principle: Fe²⁺ forms iron(II) hydroxide, which is greenish.
steps: Adding NaOH to Fe²⁺ solution forms Fe(OH)₂.
This precipitate is usually described as dirty green or green.
On standing, it may darken as oxidation occurs.
Therefore the precipitate is green.
explanation.simple_explanation: Fe²⁺ commonly gives a green hydroxide precipitate with NaOH. Check our "Tutorial section" for qualitative analysis.

subtopic: Iron: tests for Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺
question: A solution containing Fe³⁺ gives with aqueous NaOH a precipitate that is usually

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the colour of the precipitate formed from Fe³⁺ with sodium hydroxide.
explanation.principle: Fe³⁺ forms iron(III) hydroxide, which is brown.
steps: When NaOH is added to Fe³⁺ solution, Fe(OH)₃ forms.
This hydroxide precipitate is reddish-brown or brown.
This helps distinguish Fe³⁺ from Fe²⁺.
Therefore the precipitate is brown.
explanation.simple_explanation: Fe³⁺ commonly gives a brown hydroxide precipitate with NaOH. Check our "Tutorial section" for ion tests.

question: Compared with pure iron, steel is often preferred in construction because it is generally

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why steel is often preferred to pure iron.
explanation.principle: Alloying changes and often improves the mechanical properties of metals.
steps: Steel contains iron plus carbon and sometimes other elements.
This makes it usually stronger and harder than pure iron.
That improved mechanical performance is useful in construction.
Therefore steel is often preferred because it is stronger and more useful mechanically.
explanation.final_answer: B. stronger and more useful mechanically
explanation.simple_explanation: Alloying iron into steel improves many practical properties. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloy uses.

subtopic: Iron: extraction from sulphide and oxide ores
question: An ore from which iron may be obtained is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an iron ore formula.
explanation.principle: Fe₂O₃ is iron(III) oxide, a common iron ore called haematite.
steps: Iron ores are compounds from which iron can be extracted economically.
Fe₂O₃ is one of the major iron ores.
This makes it a standard formula to remember.
Therefore the ore is Fe₂O₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Memorizing common ore formulas helps with extraction questions. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: A major reason iron is widely used in machinery is that it

options.B: has useful strength and can form improved alloys

answer_text: has useful strength and can form improved alloys

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why iron is widely used in machinery.
explanation.principle: Engineering materials need strength and suitable mechanical behavior.
steps: Iron is strong and available in large quantities.
It also forms useful alloys such as steels.
These materials can be tailored for different mechanical purposes.
Therefore iron is widely used because it has useful strength and can form improved alloys.
explanation.final_answer: B. has useful strength and can form improved alloys
explanation.simple_explanation: Mechanical strength and alloy formation make iron very important industrially. Check our "Tutorial section" for applied metal chemistry.

subtopic: Copper: extraction from sulphide and oxide ores
question: A common sulphide ore of copper is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common sulphide ore of copper.
explanation.principle: Chalcopyrite is one of the best known copper ores.
steps: It is a sulphide ore containing copper and iron.
It is an important commercial source of copper.
This makes it a standard ore name to remember.
Therefore the correct answer is chalcopyrite.
explanation.simple_explanation: Chalcopyrite is a common sulphide ore of copper. Check our "Tutorial section" for ore names.

subtopic: Copper: extraction from sulphide and oxide ores
question: Copper can be obtained from copper(II) oxide by heating it with

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a suitable reducing agent for copper(II) oxide.
explanation.principle: Copper is low enough in the reactivity series to be obtained by reduction with carbon-based reducing agents.
steps: Copper(II) oxide contains oxygen combined with copper.
Carbon or carbon(II) oxide can remove this oxygen.
Metallic copper is then obtained.
Therefore copper can be obtained by heating copper(II) oxide with carbon or carbon(II) oxide.
explanation.simple_explanation: Less reactive metal oxides can often be reduced by carbon or CO. Check our "Tutorial section" for extraction methods.

question: Copper is widely used for electrical wiring mainly because it

options.A: is a poor conductor but very heavy
options.B: conducts electricity well and is ductile

answer_text: conducts electricity well and is ductile

explanation.question_restate: We are to connect copper’s properties with one of its major uses.
explanation.principle: Electrical wiring needs a material that conducts electricity well and can be drawn into wires.
steps: Copper has high electrical conductivity.
It is also ductile, so it can be drawn into long wires.
These two properties make it especially useful for electrical work.
Therefore the correct answer is that it conducts electricity well and is ductile.
explanation.final_answer: B. conducts electricity well and is ductile
explanation.simple_explanation: Copper’s conductivity and ductility explain its importance in wiring. Check our "Tutorial section" for uses of metals.

question: One reason copper is used in cooking utensils is that it

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain a practical use of copper.
explanation.principle: Cooking utensils require efficient heat transfer.
steps: Copper transfers heat very effectively.
This helps food heat more evenly and quickly.
That is why copper is valuable in some cookware.
Therefore one reason is that it conducts heat well.
explanation.simple_explanation: Thermal conductivity is a major reason copper is useful in cooking utensils. Check our "Tutorial section" for property–use links.

question: Copper(II) tetraoxosulphate(VI) has the formula

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the formula of copper(II) tetraoxosulphate(VI).
explanation.principle: This is the systematic name for copper(II) sulphate.
steps: The tetraoxosulphate(VI) ion is SO₄²⁻.
Copper(II) is Cu²⁺.
A 1:1 combination gives CuSO₄.
Therefore the formula is CuSO₄.
explanation.simple_explanation: Balancing ionic charges helps in writing formulas correctly. Check our "Tutorial section" for formula writing.

question: Hydrated copper(II) tetraoxosulphate(VI) crystals are commonly

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the common appearance of hydrated copper(II) sulphate crystals.
explanation.principle: Hydrated CuSO₄·5H₂O is well known for its blue colour.
steps: Water of crystallization affects the appearance of many salts.
Hydrated copper(II) sulphate crystals are bright blue.
When strongly heated, they lose water and become white.
Therefore the hydrated crystals are blue.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrated copper(II) sulphate is a classic blue salt in school chemistry. Check our "Tutorial section" for hydrated salts.

question: A solution containing Cu²⁺ usually gives with aqueous NaOH a precipitate that is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the colour of the precipitate formed by Cu²⁺ with sodium hydroxide.
explanation.principle: Copper(II) hydroxide is a blue precipitate.
steps: When NaOH is added to Cu²⁺ solution, Cu(OH)₂ forms.
This precipitate is characteristically blue.
That helps in qualitative identification of copper(II) ions.
Therefore the precipitate is blue.
explanation.simple_explanation: Cu²⁺ commonly gives a blue hydroxide precipitate. Check our "Tutorial section" for ion tests.

question: When excess aqueous ammonia is added to a Cu²⁺ solution, a deep blue solution forms because of

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why Cu²⁺ with excess ammonia gives a deep blue solution.
explanation.principle: Ammonia can act as a ligand and form a complex ion with copper(II).
steps: Initially a blue precipitate may form in some cases.
In excess ammonia, the copper(II) ion forms a soluble deep-blue complex.
This is an example of complex-ion formation.
Therefore the deep blue solution forms because of complex ion formation.
explanation.simple_explanation: The deep-blue ammonia complex of copper(II) is very important in qualitative analysis. Check our "Tutorial section" for complex ions.

question: Which statement about copper is correct?

options.A: it is widely used for electrical wiring
options.B: it is the main constituent of limestone

answer_text: it is widely used for electrical wiring

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a correct statement about copper.
explanation.principle: Copper is one of the most important conducting metals in practical use.
steps: Because it conducts electricity well and is ductile, copper is used in wires.
Limestone is associated with calcium compounds, not copper.
Copper also forms alloys such as brass and bronze.
Therefore the correct statement is that it is widely used for electrical wiring.
explanation.final_answer: A. it is widely used for electrical wiring
explanation.simple_explanation: Copper is very important in electrical applications. Check our "Tutorial section" for uses of metals.

question: Copper is often preferred for electric cables rather than iron because copper

options.A: is a poorer conductor but cheaper
options.B: conducts electricity better and resists corrosion better
options.C: is a gas at room temperature

answer_text: conducts electricity better and resists corrosion better

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare copper with iron for electrical use.
explanation.principle: Good electrical materials should conduct well and last a long time.
steps: Copper has higher electrical conductivity than iron.
It also resists corrosion better in many ordinary conditions.
These advantages make it very suitable for cables.
Therefore copper is preferred because it conducts electricity better and resists corrosion better.
explanation.final_answer: B. conducts electricity better and resists corrosion better
explanation.simple_explanation: Practical material choice depends on a combination of useful properties. Check our "Tutorial section" for comparisons.

subtopic: Alloys: steel / stainless steel / brass / bronze
question: Steel is best described as

options.B: an alloy of iron with carbon and sometimes other elements

answer_text: an alloy of iron with carbon and sometimes other elements

explanation.question_restate: We are to define steel correctly.
explanation.principle: Steel is not pure iron but an alloy based on iron.
steps: Small amounts of carbon are added to iron to make steel.
Other elements may also be present depending on the type of steel.
This improves many mechanical properties.
Therefore steel is an alloy of iron with carbon and sometimes other elements.
explanation.final_answer: B. an alloy of iron with carbon and sometimes other elements
explanation.simple_explanation: Steel is an alloy, not pure iron. Check our "Tutorial section" for common alloys.

question: Stainless steel differs from ordinary steel mainly because it contains chromium which helps

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why chromium is important in stainless steel.
explanation.principle: Chromium improves resistance to rusting and chemical attack.
steps: A protective chromium oxide layer can form on the surface.
This helps prevent further corrosion.
That is why stainless steel is more corrosion-resistant than ordinary steel.
Therefore chromium helps increase corrosion resistance.
explanation.simple_explanation: Chromium is central to the corrosion resistance of stainless steel. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloy design.

question: Brass is mainly an alloy of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main composition of brass.
explanation.principle: Brass is one of the best-known copper alloys.
steps: Copper is the major component of brass.
Zinc is the main alloying addition.
This gives brass useful properties for ornaments and fittings.
Therefore brass is mainly an alloy of copper and zinc.
explanation.simple_explanation: Brass is a copper–zinc alloy. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloy names.

question: Bronze is mainly an alloy of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main composition of bronze.
explanation.principle: Bronze is a traditional copper alloy containing tin.
steps: Copper forms the major part of bronze.
Tin is the important alloying element.
This makes bronze different from brass, which contains zinc.
Therefore bronze is mainly an alloy of copper and tin.
explanation.simple_explanation: Bronze is a copper–tin alloy. Check our "Tutorial section" for comparing alloys.

question: Soft solder is commonly associated with an alloy of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the alloy commonly used as soft solder in school chemistry.
explanation.principle: Soft solder is a low-melting alloy traditionally based on tin and lead.
steps: Solder is used to join metal surfaces.
A low melting point is important for this function.
Tin–lead alloys have historically been widely used for soft solder.
Therefore soft solder is associated with tin and lead.
explanation.simple_explanation: Soft solder is classically described as a tin–lead alloy. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloy uses.

question: Duralumin is associated mainly with aluminium because it is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify what kind of alloy duralumin is.
explanation.principle: Duralumin is a well-known alloy based on aluminium.
steps: Aluminium is the major component of duralumin.
Other elements are added to improve strength.
This helps explain its use in light structures.
Therefore duralumin is an aluminium-based alloy.
explanation.simple_explanation: Duralumin is one of the important aluminium alloys. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloy examples.

question: Permalloy is especially known for

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an important area of use for permalloy.
explanation.principle: Permalloy is a magnetic alloy valued in electrical and magnetic devices.
steps: It has special magnetic properties.
These make it useful in transformers and other magnetic applications.
The name is often linked with soft magnetic behavior.
Therefore permalloy is especially known for magnetic applications.
explanation.simple_explanation: Permalloy is remembered mainly for magnetic uses. Check our "Tutorial section" for special alloys.

question: Alnico is commonly associated with the manufacture of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common use of alnico.
explanation.principle: Alnico is a magnetic alloy used where strong permanent magnet properties are needed.
steps: The name comes from aluminium, nickel and cobalt as key components.
Its magnetic properties make it suitable for magnets.
That is why it is used in magnetic devices.
Therefore alnico is commonly associated with the manufacture of permanent magnets.
explanation.simple_explanation: Alnico is a classic magnetic alloy. Check our "Tutorial section" for named alloys and uses.

question: Alloys are generally preferred to pure metals in many uses because they often are

options.B: harder or more resistant to corrosion

answer_text: harder or more resistant to corrosion

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why alloys are so important in practical applications.
explanation.principle: Mixing metals can improve their properties.
steps: Alloys can be made harder than the pure metal.
They can also be more resistant to corrosion or wear.
This makes them more useful in tools, machines and structures.
Therefore alloys are often preferred because they are harder or more resistant to corrosion.
explanation.final_answer: B. harder or more resistant to corrosion
explanation.simple_explanation: Alloying is done to improve useful properties. Check our "Tutorial section" for materials chemistry.

subtopic: Alloys: steel versus iron / stainless steel / brass / bronze
question: Which pair is correctly matched?

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the correctly matched alloy and composition.
explanation.principle: Knowing the standard compositions of common alloys is important.
steps: Brass is copper and zinc.
Bronze is mainly copper and tin.
Steel is based on iron and carbon.
Therefore the correctly matched pair is brass — copper and zinc.
explanation.simple_explanation: Alloy names and compositions are common objective-question material. Check our "Tutorial section" for revision tables.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: A metal likely to be extracted by electrolysis rather than carbon reduction is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a metal whose extraction requires electrolysis.
explanation.principle: Highly reactive metals usually cannot be extracted from their compounds by simple carbon reduction.
steps: Aluminium is high in the reactivity series.
Its oxide is very stable.
So electrolysis is used for extraction.
Therefore aluminium is extracted by electrolysis rather than carbon reduction.
explanation.simple_explanation: Use the reactivity series to predict extraction method. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which metal ion is most likely to give a white precipitate with NaOH that dissolves in excess NaOH?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the ion showing amphoteric hydroxide behavior with sodium hydroxide.
explanation.principle: Aluminium hydroxide is amphoteric and dissolves in excess alkali.
steps: A small amount of NaOH gives a white precipitate of Al(OH)₃.
Because Al(OH)₃ is amphoteric, excess NaOH dissolves it.
Cu²⁺, Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ behave differently in this test.
Therefore the ion is Al³⁺.
explanation.simple_explanation: Amphoterism is a very useful clue in qualitative analysis. Check our "Tutorial section" for ion tests.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which pair is correctly matched with its common use?

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the correct metal or compound paired with a familiar use.
explanation.principle: Copper is widely known for its role in electrical systems.
steps: Copper conducts electricity very well.
It is also ductile enough to be drawn into wires.
That makes electrical wiring one of its most important uses.
Therefore the correct pair is copper — electrical wiring.
explanation.simple_explanation: Many objective questions link substances directly to their uses. Check our "Tutorial section" for quick summaries.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which pair of precipitate colours is correctly matched?

options.A: Fe²⁺ with NaOH — green; Cu²⁺ with NaOH — blue
options.B: Fe³⁺ with NaOH — blue; Cu²⁺ with NaOH — green
options.C: Al³⁺ with NaOH — brown; Fe²⁺ with NaOH — white
options.D: Ca²⁺ with NaOH — deep blue; Fe³⁺ with NaOH — yellow

answer_text: Fe²⁺ with NaOH — green; Cu²⁺ with NaOH — blue

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the correct colour matches from common ion tests.
explanation.principle: Different metal ions often give different hydroxide precipitate colours.
steps: Fe²⁺ commonly gives a green precipitate with NaOH.
Cu²⁺ commonly gives a blue precipitate with NaOH.
These colour differences help in qualitative identification.
Therefore the correctly matched pair is Fe²⁺ — green; Cu²⁺ — blue.
explanation.final_answer: A. Fe²⁺ with NaOH — green; Cu²⁺ with NaOH — blue
explanation.simple_explanation: Learning precipitate colours is essential for exam-style qualitative analysis. Check our "Tutorial section" for a revision table.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which process is correctly matched to its product?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correctly matched industrial process and product.
explanation.principle: Process names are often tested directly in JAMB-style chemistry questions.
steps: The Solvay process is used to make sodium carbonate, Na₂CO₃.
The Haber process makes ammonia, not NaOH.
The contact process makes sulphuric acid, not aluminium.
Therefore the correct match is Solvay process — Na₂CO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Named industrial processes are important exam material. Check our "Tutorial section" for process summaries.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which pair of substances is best associated with mortar setting?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the substances involved in the hardening of mortar.
explanation.principle: Mortar setting involves calcium hydroxide reacting with carbon(IV) oxide from the air.
steps: Slaked lime in mortar is Ca(OH)₂.
Air supplies CO₂.
These react to form hard calcium carbonate.
Therefore the correct pair is Ca(OH)₂ and CO₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Mortar sets because calcium hydroxide reacts with carbon dioxide. Check our "Tutorial section" for construction chemistry.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which statement correctly compares brass and bronze?

options.A: brass contains copper and zinc, while bronze contains copper and tin
options.B: brass contains copper and tin, while bronze contains copper and zinc

answer_text: brass contains copper and zinc, while bronze contains copper and tin

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare two common copper alloys.
explanation.principle: Brass and bronze are often confused, so their compositions should be memorized clearly.
steps: Brass is the copper–zinc alloy.
Bronze is mainly the copper–tin alloy.
This difference gives them somewhat different properties and uses.
Therefore the correct statement is that brass contains copper and zinc, while bronze contains copper and tin.
explanation.final_answer: A. brass contains copper and zinc, while bronze contains copper and tin
explanation.simple_explanation: Brass and bronze are classic alloy-comparison questions. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloy tables.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: A metal likely to be chosen for an electrical cable and also to form brass is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the metal that fits both uses described.
explanation.principle: Copper is central both to electrical wiring and to the alloy brass.
steps: Copper conducts electricity very well, so it is used in cables.
Brass is mainly copper plus zinc.
This means copper satisfies both parts of the question.
Therefore the correct answer is copper.
explanation.simple_explanation: Look for the one substance that fits every clue in the question. Check our "Tutorial section" for mixed revision.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which ion is most likely to be identified by a brick-red flame test?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the ion associated with the brick-red flame colour.
explanation.principle: Certain metal ions give distinctive flame colours.
steps: Calcium ions give a brick-red flame.
Sodium gives a yellow flame, while some others are not identified mainly by this flame colour at this level.
Therefore the ion is Ca²⁺.
This makes flame tests useful in qualitative chemistry.
explanation.simple_explanation: Calcium gives a brick-red flame. Check our "Tutorial section" for flame tests.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which statement best summarizes why alloys are often preferred to pure metals in engineering?

options.A: alloys are always lighter than hydrogen
options.B: alloys often have improved mechanical and corrosion-resistant properties

answer_text: alloys often have improved mechanical and corrosion-resistant properties

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why alloys are so important in engineering and technology.
explanation.principle: Mixing metals or metals with small amounts of other elements often improves useful properties.
steps: Alloys may be harder than the pure metal.
They may also resist corrosion better or have better strength.
This makes them more suitable for machines, tools and structures.
Therefore alloys are often preferred because they have improved mechanical and corrosion-resistant properties.
explanation.final_answer: B. alloys often have improved mechanical and corrosion-resistant properties
explanation.simple_explanation: Engineering materials are chosen for performance, not just composition. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloys and applications.

question: A property that explains why many metals are used for electrical cables is their

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the property that makes metals suitable for electrical cables.
explanation.principle: Metals contain mobile electrons which carry electric charge through the structure.
steps: Electrical cables must allow current to flow easily.
In metals, delocalized electrons move freely.
This movement allows efficient conduction of electricity.
Therefore many metals are used for cables because of high electrical conductivity.
explanation.simple_explanation: Free-moving electrons make many metals good conductors. Check our "Tutorial section" to learn more.

question: The ability of a metal to be hammered into sheets without breaking is called

explanation.question_restate: We are to name the property that allows a metal to be hammered into thin sheets.
explanation.principle: This property is one of the classic physical properties of many metals.
steps: When force is applied to a metal, layers of metal atoms can slide.
The metallic bonding is not completely destroyed.
That is why the metal changes shape instead of shattering.
Therefore the property is called malleability.
explanation.simple_explanation: Malleability means a metal can be hammered into sheets. Check our "Tutorial section" for metal properties.

question: A metal is described as ductile if it can be

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the meaning of ductility.
explanation.principle: Ductility is another common physical property of metals.
steps: Wires need a material that can be stretched out without snapping quickly.
Many metals show this behavior.
That is why copper is used in electrical wiring.
Therefore ductile means can be drawn into wires.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ductility means a metal can be drawn into wires. Check our "Tutorial section" for common properties.

question: A freshly polished metal surface is usually shiny because metals possess

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the name given to the shiny appearance of metals.
explanation.principle: Many metals reflect light strongly when freshly cut or polished.
steps: A shiny metallic surface is a visible physical property.
This property is called lustre.
It is one reason many metals are attractive for decorative uses.
Therefore the shiny appearance is called lustre.
explanation.simple_explanation: Lustre is the shine seen on many metallic surfaces. Check our "Tutorial section" for quick revision.

question: Most metals form positive ions because they tend to

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why metals usually form cations.
explanation.principle: Metals generally have relatively low tendency to hold onto outer electrons compared with many non-metals.
steps: When a metal atom reacts, it often loses one or more outer electrons.
This leaves the atom with a net positive charge.
A positively charged ion is called a cation.
Therefore metals tend to form positive ions because they lose electrons.
explanation.simple_explanation: Metals usually become cations by losing electrons. Check our "Tutorial section" for ionic bonding.

question: The good heat conductivity of metals is mainly due to

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why metals transfer heat efficiently.
explanation.principle: The same delocalized electrons that help in electrical conduction also help in heat conduction.
steps: Heating one part of a metal makes particles and electrons gain energy.
The mobile electrons move and transfer this energy through the metal rapidly.
This causes heat to spread quickly.
Therefore metals conduct heat well because of mobile electrons transferring energy quickly.
explanation.final_answer: B. mobile electrons transferring energy quickly
explanation.simple_explanation: Free electrons help explain heat conduction in metals. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallic bonding.

question: Which of the following is NOT a general property of metals?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify what is not generally true of metals.
explanation.principle: Many metals share several common physical properties, but not every hard substance property applies to them.
steps: Metals are commonly lustrous, sonorous and good conductors of electricity.
Many are also malleable and ductile.
Brittleness is not a universal metallic property.
Therefore universal brittleness is NOT a general property of metals.
explanation.simple_explanation: Focus on the usual metallic properties instead of exceptions. Check our "Tutorial section" for summaries.

question: Why are many metals sonorous?

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why many metals make a ringing sound when struck.
explanation.principle: Sound is produced when vibrations are created and passed through a material.
steps: When a metal is struck, vibrations are set up in the solid.
These vibrations can pass efficiently through the rigid metallic structure.
That produces the ringing sound called sonority.
Therefore metals are often sonorous because their structures transmit vibrations effectively.
explanation.final_answer: B. their structures transmit vibrations effectively
explanation.simple_explanation: Sonority is the ringing sound many metals make when struck. Check our "Tutorial section" for more.

question: The fact that metals can mix with one another to form alloys is useful because alloys often have

options.D: identical composition to the pure metals

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify why alloy formation is important.
explanation.principle: Alloys are often produced to improve the useful properties of a metal.
steps: Different metal atoms can share a metallic structure.
This can make the material harder, stronger, or more corrosion-resistant.
That is why alloys are widely used in engineering and industry.
Therefore alloys often have improved properties.
explanation.simple_explanation: Alloying is done to improve performance. Check our "Tutorial section" for materials chemistry.

question: Which statement best explains metallic bonding?

options.A: positive ions are held together by a sea of delocalized electrons
options.B: negative ions are joined by shared protons
options.C: molecules are held by hydrogen bonds only
options.D: atoms are held only by ionic bonds with water

answer_text: positive ions are held together by a sea of delocalized electrons

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the best description of metallic bonding.
explanation.principle: Metallic bonding is different from simple ionic or covalent bonding.
steps: In a metal, atoms lose some control of their outer electrons.
These electrons become delocalized and move through the structure.
The positive metal ions are held together by attraction to this electron sea.
Therefore metallic bonding is best described as positive ions held together by a sea of delocalized electrons.
explanation.final_answer: A. positive ions are held together by a sea of delocalized electrons
explanation.simple_explanation: Metallic bonding explains many physical properties of metals. Check our "Tutorial section" for bonding models.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium hydroxide / electrolysis of brine
question: In the chlor-alkali industry, sodium hydroxide is obtained from
