explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the industrial source of sodium hydroxide.
explanation.principle: NaOH is one of the major products of electrolysis of brine.
steps: Brine is concentrated sodium chloride solution.
During electrolysis, chlorine forms at the anode and hydrogen at the cathode.
The remaining solution becomes rich in sodium hydroxide.
Therefore sodium hydroxide is obtained from electrolysis of brine.
explanation.simple_explanation: NaOH is a major chlor-alkali product. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial processes.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: electrolysis of brine
question: In the electrolysis of brine, hydrogen is evolved at the

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the electrode where hydrogen is formed in brine electrolysis.
explanation.principle: Reduction takes place at the cathode.
steps: At the cathode, water molecules are reduced in the aqueous system.
This produces hydrogen gas.
At the anode, chloride ions are oxidized to chlorine.
Therefore hydrogen is evolved at the cathode.
explanation.simple_explanation: Brine electrolysis gives hydrogen at the cathode. Check our "Tutorial section" for electrode processes.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) / Solvay process
question: The Solvay process is used in the industrial preparation of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the product of the Solvay process.
explanation.principle: The Solvay process is the standard industrial method for sodium carbonate production.
steps: Sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) is sodium carbonate, Na₂CO₃.
This compound is used in glass manufacture and other industries.
The Solvay process is the named route for making it.
Therefore the product is Na₂CO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Match process names with products carefully. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial chemistry.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium trioxocarbonate(IV)
question: Sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) is commonly called

explanation.question_restate: We are to match the compound Na₂CO₃ with its common name.
explanation.principle: Sodium carbonate has a widely used domestic and industrial name.
steps: The formula Na₂CO₃ belongs to sodium carbonate.
Its common name is washing soda.
This is different from NaOH, which is caustic soda.
Therefore sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) is washing soda.
explanation.simple_explanation: Know both common names and systematic names. Check our "Tutorial section" for naming practice.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium hydrogen trioxocarbonate(IV)
question: Sodium hydrogen trioxocarbonate(IV) has the formula

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct formula of sodium hydrogen trioxocarbonate(IV).
explanation.principle: This compound is sodium hydrogencarbonate.
steps: The hydrogencarbonate ion is HCO₃⁻.
Combining Na⁺ with HCO₃⁻ gives NaHCO₃.
This distinguishes it from sodium carbonate, Na₂CO₃.
Therefore the formula is NaHCO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogencarbonates contain HCO₃⁻, not CO₃²⁻. Check our "Tutorial section" for formula writing.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium chloride
question: Sodium chloride is best described as

options.B: an ionic compound of Na⁺ and Cl⁻

answer_text: an ionic compound of Na⁺ and Cl⁻

explanation.question_restate: We are to describe the bonding in sodium chloride correctly.
explanation.principle: Sodium chloride forms by transfer of an electron from sodium to chlorine.
steps: Sodium loses one electron to become Na⁺.
Chlorine gains one electron to become Cl⁻.
The electrostatic attraction between these ions produces the compound.
Therefore sodium chloride is an ionic compound of Na⁺ and Cl⁻.
explanation.final_answer: B. an ionic compound of Na⁺ and Cl⁻
explanation.simple_explanation: Ionic bonding helps explain the composition of common salt. Check our "Tutorial section" for bonding basics.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium hydroxide
question: The common name of sodium hydroxide is

explanation.question_restate: We are to match sodium hydroxide with its common name.
explanation.principle: NaOH is widely known as caustic soda.
steps: Sodium hydroxide is a strong alkali.
It is used in soap making and other industries.
Its common name is caustic soda.
Therefore the correct answer is caustic soda.
explanation.simple_explanation: Common names are often tested alongside formulas. Check our "Tutorial section" for names of compounds.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: economic importance of sea water
question: Sea water is economically important mainly because it is a source of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a reason sea water is economically important.
explanation.principle: Sea water contains dissolved salts that can be recovered and used.
steps: The most familiar one is sodium chloride.
Other dissolved salts and useful minerals may also be obtained.
This makes sea water valuable as a natural resource.
Therefore it is economically important because it is a source of useful salts.
explanation.simple_explanation: Sea water has economic value because of the salts dissolved in it. Check our "Tutorial section" for applied chemistry.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: Solvay process
question: An important raw material used in the Solvay process is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a raw material used in the Solvay process.
explanation.principle: The Solvay process depends on sodium chloride solution as a starting material.
steps: Brine is concentrated sodium chloride solution.
It provides the sodium ions needed for sodium carbonate production.
This makes it one of the key raw materials of the process.
Therefore the correct answer is brine.
explanation.simple_explanation: Brine is central both to the Solvay process and the chlor-alkali industry. Check our "Tutorial section" for process summaries.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) / uses
question: A major use of sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) is in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an important use of sodium carbonate.
explanation.principle: Sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) has several industrial uses, especially in glass making.
steps: It helps lower the melting point of silica mixtures in glass production.
This improves the manufacturing process.
That is why sodium carbonate is a key industrial chemical.
Therefore a major use is in glass manufacture.
explanation.simple_explanation: Many compound uses follow directly from industrial process needs. Check our "Tutorial section" for uses of sodium compounds.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium oxide from sea shells
question: Quicklime is obtained by heating sea shells which contain mainly

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main calcium compound present in sea shells.
explanation.principle: Sea shells are largely made of calcium carbonate.
steps: Sea shells are natural sources of CaCO₃.
On strong heating, CaCO₃ decomposes to CaO and CO₂.
The CaO obtained is quicklime.
Therefore sea shells contain mainly CaCO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Sea shells provide calcium carbonate for quicklime preparation. Check our "Tutorial section" for calcium chemistry.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium oxide
question: The industrial or laboratory preparation of quicklime from limestone is an example of

explanation.question_restate: We are to classify the reaction used to obtain CaO from CaCO₃.
explanation.principle: Heating a substance until it breaks down into simpler substances is thermal decomposition.
steps: Calcium carbonate is heated strongly.
It breaks into calcium oxide and carbon(IV) oxide.
Because heat causes the breakdown, the reaction is a thermal decomposition.
Therefore preparation of quicklime from limestone is thermal decomposition.
explanation.simple_explanation: Thermal decomposition is a key reaction type in extraction and preparation. Check our "Tutorial section" for reaction types.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium hydroxide
question: When water is added to calcium oxide, the product formed is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the product formed when quicklime reacts with water.
explanation.principle: Calcium oxide reacts with water to form calcium hydroxide.
steps: Quicklime is CaO.
Water combines with CaO to form Ca(OH)₂.
This process is called slaking of lime.
Therefore the product formed is Ca(OH)₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Adding water to quicklime gives slaked lime. Check our "Tutorial section" for the lime cycle.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium hydroxide / limewater
question: Limewater is best described as

explanation.question_restate: We are to define limewater correctly.
explanation.principle: Limewater is the clear solution formed when a little calcium hydroxide dissolves in water.
steps: Slaked lime is Ca(OH)₂.
Only a small amount dissolves in water.
The clear solution obtained is called limewater.
Therefore limewater is an aqueous solution of Ca(OH)₂.
explanation.final_answer: B. an aqueous solution of Ca(OH)₂
explanation.simple_explanation: Limewater is the clear solution of calcium hydroxide in water. Check our "Tutorial section" for common reagents.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium trioxocarbonate(IV)
question: Passing carbon(IV) oxide through limewater produces a white precipitate of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the product of the limewater test for CO₂.
explanation.principle: Carbon(IV) oxide reacts with calcium hydroxide to form calcium carbonate.
steps: Limewater is Ca(OH)₂ solution.
When CO₂ is bubbled through it, CaCO₃ is formed.
Calcium carbonate is insoluble and appears as a white precipitate.
Therefore the product is CaCO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: The limewater test produces a white precipitate of calcium carbonate. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: cement composition
question: Cement is produced largely from a mixture containing limestone and

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify another major raw material used with limestone in cement manufacture.
explanation.principle: Cement materials come mainly from calcium-rich and silicate-rich sources.
steps: Limestone supplies calcium compounds.
Clay supplies silica and alumina.
Heating these materials together leads to cement formation.
Therefore cement is produced largely from a mixture containing limestone and clay.
explanation.simple_explanation: Cement chemistry depends strongly on limestone and clay. Check our "Tutorial section" for building materials.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: setting of mortar
question: Mortar hardens mainly because Ca(OH)₂ reacts with

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why mortar becomes hard on standing.
explanation.principle: Slaked lime in mortar reacts with carbon dioxide to reform calcium carbonate.
steps: Mortar contains calcium hydroxide, Ca(OH)₂.
The air supplies CO₂.
These react to form CaCO₃, which is hard and binding.
Therefore mortar hardens mainly because Ca(OH)₂ reacts with CO₂ from the air.
explanation.simple_explanation: Mortar sets as calcium carbonate forms again. Check our "Tutorial section" for construction chemistry.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: test for Ca²⁺
question: The common flame colour for Ca²⁺ is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the flame colour of calcium ions.
explanation.principle: Flame tests are useful for identifying some metal ions.
steps: Different ions emit different characteristic colours in a flame.
Calcium gives a brick-red or orange-red flame.
This helps distinguish it from sodium or potassium.
Therefore the common flame colour for Ca²⁺ is brick-red.
explanation.simple_explanation: Calcium gives a brick-red flame in flame tests. Check our "Tutorial section" for flame colours.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium oxide / calcium hydroxide
question: Which sequence correctly represents the lime cycle?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct order of conversion from limestone to slaked lime.
explanation.principle: Heating and then addition of water give the familiar lime cycle sequence.
steps: Calcium carbonate is first heated to produce calcium oxide.
This calcium oxide is quicklime.
Adding water gives calcium hydroxide, which is slaked lime.
Therefore the correct sequence is CaCO₃ → CaO → Ca(OH)₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: The lime cycle is a very useful sequence to remember. Check our "Tutorial section" for calcium compounds.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium trioxocarbonate(IV)
question: Calcium trioxocarbonate(IV) is commonly known as

explanation.question_restate: We are to match calcium trioxocarbonate(IV) with a common name.
explanation.principle: CaCO₃ occurs naturally as limestone and in sea shells.
steps: The systematic name is calcium trioxocarbonate(IV).
A common natural form is limestone.
This compound is important in cement, lime and building chemistry.
Therefore calcium trioxocarbonate(IV) is commonly known as limestone.
explanation.simple_explanation: Limestone is a common natural form of CaCO₃. Check our "Tutorial section" for common names.

question: Bauxite is purified chiefly to obtain

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the useful aluminium compound obtained after purifying bauxite.
explanation.principle: The purified material is alumina, which is aluminium oxide.
steps: Bauxite contains aluminium compounds plus impurities.
Purification removes unwanted substances.
The useful product for extraction is alumina, Al₂O₃.
Therefore bauxite is purified chiefly to obtain alumina.
explanation.simple_explanation: Purified alumina is the material used in aluminium extraction. Check our "Tutorial section" for extraction stages.

question: One important reason for purifying bauxite before extraction is to remove

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain one purpose of purifying bauxite.
explanation.principle: Natural ores usually contain substances that would interfere with efficient extraction.
steps: Bauxite often contains iron oxides and silica as impurities.
These are not useful for the extraction of aluminium.
Removing them improves efficiency and purity of the feed material.
Therefore one important reason is to remove iron oxide impurities.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ore purification removes unwanted materials that would interfere with extraction. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: Aluminium is extracted from alumina by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the extraction method used for aluminium.
explanation.principle: Highly reactive metals are often extracted by electrolysis.
steps: Aluminium oxide is too stable to be reduced easily by carbon.
So electrolysis is used instead.
The process releases aluminium at the cathode.
Therefore aluminium is extracted from alumina by electrolysis.
explanation.simple_explanation: Reactive metals often require electrolysis for extraction. Check our "Tutorial section" for extraction methods.

question: The reason carbon reduction is not normally used for aluminium extraction is that aluminium

options.A: is too low in the reactivity series
options.B: is highly reactive and its oxide is very stable

answer_text: is highly reactive and its oxide is very stable

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why aluminium extraction does not usually use carbon reduction.
explanation.principle: The reactivity of the metal and the stability of its oxide determine the method used.
steps: Aluminium is high in the reactivity series.
Its oxide, Al₂O₃, is very stable.
Carbon is not used in the ordinary way to reduce it economically.
Therefore aluminium is extracted electrolytically because it is highly reactive and its oxide is very stable.
explanation.final_answer: B. is highly reactive and its oxide is very stable
explanation.simple_explanation: Use the reactivity series to explain extraction methods. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: A major property of aluminium that makes it useful in aircraft manufacture is its

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a major useful property of aluminium.
explanation.principle: Aircraft materials benefit from being light in mass.
steps: Aluminium is relatively light compared with many structural metals.
This reduces total weight in vehicles and aircraft.
When alloyed, it can still be strong.
Therefore a major useful property is its low density.
explanation.simple_explanation: Low density is one of aluminium’s most useful properties. Check our "Tutorial section" for applications.

question: Aluminium resists corrosion mainly because it forms

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why aluminium is corrosion resistant.
explanation.principle: A thin oxide film forms on aluminium and protects it from further attack.
steps: When aluminium is exposed to air, its surface oxidizes.
This produces a thin layer of Al₂O₃.
The layer sticks well and protects the metal beneath.
Therefore aluminium resists corrosion because it forms a protective oxide layer.
explanation.simple_explanation: The aluminium oxide film is a key reason for corrosion resistance. Check our "Tutorial section" for metal protection.

question: A common domestic use of aluminium follows from the fact that it is

explanation.question_restate: We are to link one property of aluminium to a household use.
explanation.principle: Cooking utensils and similar items need efficient transfer of heat.
steps: Aluminium conducts heat well.
That makes it useful in pots, pans and cooking vessels.
It is also fairly light and corrosion resistant.
Therefore a common domestic use follows from the fact that it is a good conductor of heat.
explanation.final_answer: A. a good conductor of heat
explanation.simple_explanation: Property–use links are very important in objective chemistry questions. Check our "Tutorial section" for examples.

question: When NaOH is added to Al³⁺ solution, the white precipitate formed is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the precipitate formed when hydroxide ions react with Al³⁺.
explanation.principle: Metal ions often form hydroxide precipitates with aqueous sodium hydroxide.
steps: Aluminium ions combine with hydroxide ions to form aluminium hydroxide.
This precipitate is white.
Because it is amphoteric, it dissolves in excess NaOH.
Therefore the white precipitate formed is Al(OH)₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Aluminium hydroxide is a white precipitate that dissolves in excess NaOH. Check our "Tutorial section" for ion tests.

question: A characteristic behavior of Al³⁺ with excess NaOH is that the precipitate

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify what happens when excess NaOH is added after the first Al³⁺ precipitate forms.
explanation.principle: Aluminium hydroxide is amphoteric and dissolves in excess alkali.
steps: At first, Al(OH)₃ forms as a white precipitate.
Because this hydroxide is amphoteric, more NaOH can dissolve it.
This is a useful distinguishing test for aluminium ions.
Therefore the precipitate dissolves.
explanation.simple_explanation: The dissolving of Al(OH)₃ in excess alkali is an important qualitative-analysis clue. Check our "Tutorial section" for amphoteric hydroxides.

question: The substance finally electrolyzed to obtain aluminium is purified

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the aluminium-containing substance used in the electrolytic cell.
explanation.principle: After bauxite is purified, the useful oxide obtained is alumina.
steps: Alumina is Al₂O₃.
It is the oxide from which aluminium is extracted electrolytically.
This makes it the final purified feed material.
Therefore the substance electrolyzed is purified alumina.
explanation.simple_explanation: Alumina is the purified oxide used in aluminium extraction. Check our "Tutorial section" for extraction steps.

question: Cassiterite is an ore of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the metal obtained from cassiterite.
explanation.principle: Cassiterite is the chief ore from which tin is extracted.
steps: Cassiterite is mainly tin(IV) oxide.
It is the standard commercial ore of tin.
That makes it important in metallurgy.
Therefore cassiterite is an ore of tin.
explanation.simple_explanation: Cassiterite is the chief ore of tin. Check our "Tutorial section" for ore names.

question: The formula of cassiterite is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the chemical formula of cassiterite.
explanation.principle: Cassiterite is tin(IV) oxide.
steps: The ore contains tin in the +4 oxidation state.
The formula of tin(IV) oxide is SnO₂.
This is the common ore formula to remember.
Therefore cassiterite is SnO₂.

question: Tin is suitable for coating steel food cans mainly because it

question: One common use of tin is in

question: The protective coating of steel by tin is important mainly because it helps to

question: Why can tin be extracted by carbon reduction while aluminium generally cannot?

question: A key feature of many first transition series elements is that they possess

question: A common property of first transition series elements is that they show

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a characteristic chemical property of transition metals.
explanation.principle: Transition metals can use different numbers of electrons in bonding.
steps: The energies of the s and d electrons are relatively close.

explanation.simple_explanation: Variable oxidation states are one of the hallmarks of transition metals. Check our "Tutorial section" for examples.

question: Many ions of transition metals are coloured mainly because of

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why many transition-metal ions are coloured.
explanation.principle: Their partly filled d-orbitals allow absorption of certain wavelengths of visible light.
steps: Electrons can move between split d-energy levels.
This happens when light energy is absorbed.
The unabsorbed light gives the observed colour.
Therefore many transition-metal ions are coloured mainly because of d-electron transitions.
explanation.simple_explanation: Colour in transition-metal ions is closely tied to d-orbitals. Check our "Tutorial section" for coloured ions.

subtopic: First transition series: complex ion formation
question: A transition-metal complex is formed when a metal ion combines with

steps: Ligands may be molecules like NH₃ or ions like Cl⁻.

explanation.final_answer: A. ligands that donate electron pairs
explanation.simple_explanation: Complex-ion formation depends on ligands donating lone pairs. Check our "Tutorial section" for coordination chemistry.

question: Transition metals are often good catalysts because they

question: Which statement best defines a catalyst?

The Solvay process makes sodium carbonate, not alumina.
Therefore the correct match is electrolysis of brine — NaOH.
explanation.simple_explanation: Named industrial processes should be learned with their products. Check our "Tutorial section" for process summaries.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which ion is most likely to give a white precipitate with NaOH that dissolves in excess NaOH?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the ion showing amphoteric hydroxide behavior with sodium hydroxide.
explanation.principle: Aluminium hydroxide dissolves in excess alkali because it is amphoteric.
steps: Adding a little NaOH to Al³⁺ gives a white precipitate of Al(OH)₃.
More NaOH causes this precipitate to dissolve.
This distinguishes it from the precipitates of many other metal ions.
Therefore the ion is Al³⁺.
explanation.simple_explanation: Amphoterism is a key clue in qualitative analysis. Check our "Tutorial section" for ion tests.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which metal is especially associated with electrical wiring and also forms brass?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the metal that fits both clues in the question.
explanation.principle: Copper is used in wiring and is also the major metal in brass.
steps: Copper conducts electricity very well, so it is used in wires.
Brass is a copper–zinc alloy.
This means copper satisfies both conditions given.
Therefore the correct answer is copper.
explanation.simple_explanation: Watch for questions where one metal fits more than one clue. Check our "Tutorial section" for mixed revision.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which pair of precipitate colours is correctly matched with NaOH?

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the correctly matched pair of hydroxide precipitate colours.
explanation.principle: Precipitate colours are important in qualitative analysis.
steps: Fe²⁺ commonly gives a green precipitate with NaOH.
Cu²⁺ commonly gives a blue precipitate with NaOH.
These colour differences help identify the ions.
Therefore the correct pair is Fe²⁺ — green; Cu²⁺ — blue.
explanation.simple_explanation: Precipitate colours are very important in school chemistry tests. Check our "Tutorial section" for revision tables.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which sequence correctly connects a natural calcium source to mortar setting?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct sequence linking limestone chemistry to mortar setting.
explanation.principle: This is the lime cycle followed by carbonation during mortar hardening.
steps: Calcium carbonate is first heated to form calcium oxide.
Adding water gives calcium hydroxide.
Later, this reacts with CO₂ from the air to reform calcium carbonate.
Therefore the correct sequence is CaCO₃ → CaO → Ca(OH)₂ → CaCO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: This complete lime cycle is very useful in revision. Check our "Tutorial section" for construction chemistry.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which pair is correctly matched with a common use?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correctly matched substance and use.
explanation.principle: Some special alloys are remembered mainly by their practical applications.
steps: Alnico is a magnetic alloy associated with permanent magnets.
CuSO₄ is not used for aircraft bodies.
CaCO₃ is not the product of brine electrolysis.
Therefore the correct match is alnico — permanent magnets.
explanation.simple_explanation: Named alloys are often linked to a specific use in objective chemistry. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloy uses.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which statement correctly compares brass and bronze?

options.A: brass contains zinc while bronze contains tin
options.B: brass contains tin while bronze contains zinc

answer_text: brass contains zinc while bronze contains tin

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare the compositions of brass and bronze.
explanation.principle: These two copper alloys are often confused, so the distinction is important.
steps: Brass is mainly copper plus zinc.
Bronze is mainly copper plus tin.
This is the standard comparison taught in school chemistry.
Therefore the correct statement is that brass contains zinc while bronze contains tin.
explanation.final_answer: A. brass contains zinc while bronze contains tin
explanation.simple_explanation: Brass and bronze are classic alloy-comparison questions. Check our "Tutorial section" for common alloys.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which metal would most likely be extracted by electrolysis rather than by carbon reduction?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the metal whose reactivity makes electrolysis necessary.
explanation.principle: Highly reactive metals are usually extracted by electrolysis.
steps: Aluminium is higher in the reactivity series than iron, copper and tin.
Its oxide is very stable.
This makes carbon reduction unsuitable in the ordinary way.
Therefore aluminium is the metal most likely to be extracted by electrolysis.
explanation.simple_explanation: Use the reactivity series to predict extraction methods. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which ion is associated with a brick-red flame test?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the ion that gives a brick-red flame.
explanation.principle: Flame tests are common quick tests for certain metal ions.
steps: Calcium ions give a brick-red flame colour.
This helps identify calcium in qualitative analysis.
The other ions listed are usually recognized by different tests.
Therefore the ion is Ca²⁺.
explanation.simple_explanation: Calcium gives a brick-red flame. Check our "Tutorial section" for flame-test revision.

subtopic: Metals and their compounds: mixed revision
question: Which statement best summarizes why alloys are often preferred to pure metals in engineering?

options.A: they often have improved strength, hardness or corrosion resistance
options.D: they are always softer than pure metals

answer_text: they often have improved strength, hardness or corrosion resistance

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why alloys are so widely used instead of pure metals.
explanation.principle: The point of alloying is usually to improve practical performance.
steps: Adding other elements changes the structure of the metal.
This can increase strength, hardness or corrosion resistance.
That is why alloys are so important in machines, buildings and tools.
Therefore alloys are often preferred because they have improved strength, hardness or corrosion resistance.
explanation.final_answer: A. they often have improved strength, hardness or corrosion resistance
explanation.simple_explanation: Engineering materials are chosen for performance, not just composition. Check our "Tutorial section" for materials chemistry.

question: Metals are generally good conductors of electricity because they possess

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why metals usually conduct electricity well.
explanation.principle: Electrical conduction in metals depends on delocalized electrons.
steps: In a metal, outer electrons are not tied permanently to one atom.
These electrons move through the lattice.
Their movement carries electric charge.
Therefore metals conduct electricity because they possess free-moving electrons.
explanation.simple_explanation: Delocalized electrons explain electrical conduction in metals. Check our "Tutorial section" to learn more.

question: A property that allows a metal sheet to be beaten into shape without cracking is

explanation.question_restate: We are to name the property that allows a metal to be hammered into shape.
explanation.principle: This property describes the ability to form sheets under pressure.
steps: When a force is applied to many metals, their layers can slide.
The metallic bonding still holds the structure together.
So the metal flattens instead of breaking easily.
Therefore the property is malleability.
explanation.simple_explanation: Malleability means a metal can be hammered into sheets. Check our "Tutorial section" for common properties.

question: Which property of metals makes them suitable for saucepans and frying pans?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the metallic property useful in cooking utensils.
explanation.principle: Cooking utensils work best when heat spreads quickly through them.
steps: Metals allow heat energy to move rapidly through the material.
This helps food cook more evenly.
That is why metals such as aluminium and copper are used for utensils.
Therefore the useful property is good heat conduction.
explanation.simple_explanation: Property–use links are very important in chemistry questions. Check our "Tutorial section" for more examples.

question: Why can many metals be drawn into wires?

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why many metals can be made into wires.
explanation.principle: The correct property for wire drawing is ductility.
steps: A ductile material can be pulled into thin strands.
Many metals possess this property.
This is why copper is useful for electric wires.
Therefore metals can be drawn into wires because they are ductile.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ductility is the property used in wire making. Check our "Tutorial section" for metals and their uses.

question: The ringing sound produced when a metal is struck is described as

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the name of the ringing sound many metals make when struck.
explanation.principle: This is one of the familiar physical properties of metals.
steps: Metals transmit vibrations efficiently.
When struck, these vibrations produce a clear ringing sound.
This property is called sonority.
Therefore the correct answer is sonority.
explanation.simple_explanation: Sonority means producing a ringing sound when struck. Check our "Tutorial section" for quick revision.

question: Which statement best explains metallic lustre?

options.B: metals reflect light from their surfaces

answer_text: metals reflect light from their surfaces

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain metallic lustre.
explanation.principle: Lustre is a visual property based on light reflection.
steps: Many metals have surfaces that reflect incident light strongly.
This makes them appear shiny when clean or freshly polished.
That shiny appearance is called metallic lustre.
Therefore metallic lustre is explained by reflection of light from the surface.
explanation.final_answer: B. metals reflect light from their surfaces
explanation.simple_explanation: Lustre is simply the shiny appearance produced by light reflection. Check our "Tutorial section" for common properties.

question: Most metallic oxides are generally

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the usual nature of many metallic oxides.
explanation.principle: Metallic oxides commonly react with acids as bases do.
steps: Many metal oxides form hydroxides or basic solutions in water or react with acids to form salt and water.
This basic behaviour contrasts with many non-metal oxides.
Although there are exceptions, the general rule holds for many metals.
Therefore most metallic oxides are generally basic.
explanation.simple_explanation: Many metallic oxides are basic, though there are important exceptions like amphoteric oxides. Check our "Tutorial section" for oxide types.

question: Which statement is generally true of metals in reactions?

options.A: they gain electrons to form anions
options.B: they lose electrons to form cations
options.C: they always form acids with water

answer_text: they lose electrons to form cations

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a general chemical behaviour of metals.
explanation.principle: Metals commonly form positive ions by electron loss.
steps: Metals tend to have relatively low ionization energies.
They can therefore lose outer electrons in reactions.
This forms positively charged ions called cations.
Therefore metals generally lose electrons to form cations.
explanation.final_answer: B. they lose electrons to form cations
explanation.simple_explanation: Metals usually form positive ions by losing electrons. Check our "Tutorial section" for ionic bonding.

question: Alloys are usually preferred to pure metals because they often are

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why alloys are widely used in practice.
explanation.principle: Alloying often improves a metal’s useful properties.
steps: A pure metal may be too soft or too reactive for some jobs.
Adding other elements can improve hardness, strength or corrosion resistance.
That is why alloys are often selected in engineering.
Therefore alloys are usually preferred because they are harder and more useful.
explanation.simple_explanation: Alloys are made to improve performance. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloy chemistry.

question: The model of metallic bonding describes a metal as

options.A: neutral molecules held by hydrogen bonds
options.B: positive ions in a sea of delocalized electrons

answer_text: positive ions in a sea of delocalized electrons

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the best description of metallic bonding.
explanation.principle: Metallic bonding is distinct from ionic and covalent bonding.
steps: In metals, outer electrons are delocalized.
The remaining particles behave as positive ions in a lattice.
Electrostatic attraction between the ions and electron sea holds the metal together.
Therefore a metal is described as positive ions in a sea of delocalized electrons.
explanation.final_answer: B. positive ions in a sea of delocalized electrons
explanation.simple_explanation: This simple model explains many metallic properties. Check our "Tutorial section" for bonding.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: electrolysis of brine
question: A major industrial product of brine electrolysis is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify one major product obtained during the electrolysis of brine.
explanation.principle: Brine electrolysis gives important industrial chemicals.
steps: Brine is concentrated sodium chloride solution.
Electrolysis produces chlorine at the anode and hydrogen at the cathode.
The solution left behind contains sodium hydroxide.
Therefore a major industrial product is NaOH.
explanation.simple_explanation: Brine electrolysis is part of the chlor-alkali industry. Check our "Tutorial section" for process summaries.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: electrolysis of brine
question: The gas liberated at the anode during electrolysis of brine is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas formed at the anode in the electrolysis of brine.
explanation.principle: Anions are discharged at the anode where oxidation occurs.
steps: Chloride ions move to the anode.
They lose electrons there.
This forms chlorine gas.
Therefore the gas liberated at the anode is Cl₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Remember: chlorine at the anode, hydrogen at the cathode. Check our "Tutorial section" for electrolysis.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: Solvay process
question: The Solvay process is used to manufacture

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the product of the Solvay process.
explanation.principle: The Solvay process is one of the famous named industrial processes.
steps: It is the standard industrial process for making sodium carbonate.
Sodium carbonate is also called sodium trioxocarbonate(IV).
This compound has important industrial uses, especially in glass manufacture.
Therefore the Solvay process manufactures sodium carbonate.
explanation.simple_explanation: Learn process names with their products. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial chemistry.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium hydroxide
question: Which common name belongs to sodium hydroxide?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the common name of NaOH.
explanation.principle: Sodium hydroxide is widely known by a common industrial name.
steps: NaOH is a strong alkali.
Its common name is caustic soda.
This is different from sodium carbonate, which is washing soda.
Therefore the correct answer is caustic soda.
explanation.simple_explanation: It is useful to know both common and systematic names. Check our "Tutorial section" for compound names.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium trioxocarbonate(IV)
question: The common name for sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) is

explanation.question_restate: We are to match sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) with its common name.
explanation.principle: Sodium trioxocarbonate(IV) is sodium carbonate, Na₂CO₃.
steps: This compound is widely used domestically and industrially.
Its common name is washing soda.
It should not be confused with sodium hydroxide or sodium hydrogencarbonate.
Therefore the correct answer is washing soda.
explanation.simple_explanation: Washing soda is Na₂CO₃. Check our "Tutorial section" for common names.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium hydrogen trioxocarbonate(IV)
question: Which formula represents sodium hydrogen trioxocarbonate(IV)?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the formula of sodium hydrogencarbonate.
explanation.principle: The hydrogencarbonate ion is HCO₃⁻.
steps: Sodium gives Na⁺.
Combining Na⁺ with HCO₃⁻ gives NaHCO₃.
Na₂CO₃ is sodium carbonate, not the hydrogencarbonate.
Therefore the correct formula is NaHCO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogencarbonates contain HCO₃⁻. Check our "Tutorial section" for formula writing.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium chloride / sea water
question: Sea water is economically important because it is a major source of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify one economically important material obtained from sea water.
explanation.principle: Sea water contains dissolved salts, especially sodium chloride.
steps: Sea water can be evaporated or processed to obtain salt.
Sodium chloride has wide domestic and industrial uses.
This makes sea water an important natural resource.
Therefore sea water is a major source of sodium chloride.
explanation.simple_explanation: Sea water is a major natural source of common salt. Check our "Tutorial section" for applied chemistry.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sodium chloride
question: Sodium chloride is correctly represented as

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct formula for sodium chloride.
explanation.principle: Ionic formulas are obtained by balancing charges.
steps: Sodium forms Na⁺.
Chlorine forms Cl⁻.
One of each ion is needed to make a neutral compound.
Therefore sodium chloride is represented as NaCl.
explanation.simple_explanation: Balance ionic charges to obtain formulas. Check our "Tutorial section" for more examples.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: Solvay process / uses
question: A major industrial use of sodium carbonate is in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an important industrial use of sodium carbonate.
explanation.principle: Na₂CO₃ is a key raw material in glass production.
steps: It helps lower the melting point of silica mixtures.
This assists the manufacture of glass.
That is one reason sodium carbonate is industrially important.
Therefore a major industrial use is in glass manufacture.
explanation.simple_explanation: Sodium carbonate is very important in the glass industry. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial uses.

subtopic: Alkali metals, especially sodium: sea water
question: Besides common salt, sea water is economically useful because it also contains

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a broader reason for the economic value of sea water.
explanation.principle: Sea water contains more than just sodium chloride.
steps: Many dissolved salts and useful minerals occur in sea water.
Some of these can be recovered for industrial use.
This adds to the economic value of sea water.
Therefore it is also useful because it contains other dissolved salts and minerals.
explanation.final_answer: A. other dissolved salts and minerals
explanation.simple_explanation: Sea water has value because it contains several useful dissolved substances. Check our "Tutorial section" for applied chemistry.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium oxide
question: Quicklime is the common name for

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the compound called quicklime.
explanation.principle: Quicklime is calcium oxide.
steps: Calcium oxide has the formula CaO.
It is obtained by heating calcium carbonate strongly.
Its common name is quicklime.
Therefore quicklime is CaO.
explanation.simple_explanation: Know common names of calcium compounds clearly. Check our "Tutorial section" for calcium chemistry.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium oxide
question: Quicklime is produced from limestone by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify how quicklime is produced from limestone.
explanation.principle: Limestone is calcium carbonate, which decomposes on strong heating.
steps: The equation is CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂.
Heat causes the carbonate to break down.
The CaO formed is quicklime.
Therefore quicklime is produced by strong heating.
explanation.simple_explanation: This is a classic example of thermal decomposition. Check our "Tutorial section" for reaction types.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium hydroxide
question: Slaked lime is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the formula of slaked lime.
explanation.principle: Slaked lime is calcium hydroxide.
steps: Calcium oxide reacts with water to form calcium hydroxide.
This product is called slaked lime.
Its formula is Ca(OH)₂.
Therefore slaked lime is Ca(OH)₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Adding water to quicklime gives slaked lime. Check our "Tutorial section" for the lime cycle.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: limewater
question: Limewater is used in the laboratory to test for

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas tested with limewater.
explanation.principle: Limewater gives a characteristic milky reaction with carbon(IV) oxide.
steps: Limewater is a solution of Ca(OH)₂.
When CO₂ is passed through it, CaCO₃ forms as a white precipitate.
This makes the solution turn milky.
Therefore limewater is used to test for CO₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Limewater is the standard test solution for carbon(IV) oxide. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: calcium carbonate
question: Limestone and marble are mainly forms of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main compound in limestone and marble.
explanation.principle: Both are naturally occurring forms rich in calcium carbonate.
steps: The formula of calcium carbonate is CaCO₃.
This compound occurs naturally in limestone, marble and sea shells.
It is important in building materials and lime chemistry.
Therefore limestone and marble are mainly forms of CaCO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Limestone and marble are natural forms of calcium carbonate. Check our "Tutorial section" for common materials.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: cement composition
question: A major raw material used with limestone in cement manufacture is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify another major raw material used in cement manufacture.
explanation.principle: Cement depends on calcium compounds together with silica- and alumina-containing materials.
steps: Limestone supplies calcium compounds.
Clay supplies silica and alumina.
These are heated together during cement manufacture.
Therefore the other major raw material is clay.
explanation.simple_explanation: Limestone and clay are key cement raw materials. Check our "Tutorial section" for building materials.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: setting of mortar
question: Mortar sets when calcium hydroxide reacts with

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the setting of mortar.
explanation.principle: Calcium hydroxide slowly reacts with carbon dioxide from air.
steps: The slaked lime in mortar is Ca(OH)₂.
Carbon dioxide from air reacts with it.
The hard product formed is CaCO₃.
Therefore mortar sets when calcium hydroxide reacts with CO₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Mortar hardens because calcium carbonate reforms. Check our "Tutorial section" for construction chemistry.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: setting of mortar
question: Which equation best represents mortar setting?

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the equation for the hardening of mortar.
explanation.principle: The hardening is due to carbonation of calcium hydroxide.
steps: Mortar contains calcium hydroxide.
It reacts with carbon dioxide from air.
This forms calcium carbonate and water.
Therefore the correct equation is Ca(OH)₂ + CO₂ → CaCO₃ + H₂O.
explanation.simple_explanation: This equation explains why mortar becomes hard. Check our "Tutorial section" for the lime cycle.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: flame test
question: The characteristic flame colour of Ca²⁺ is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the flame colour of calcium ions.
explanation.principle: Flame tests help distinguish some metal ions.
steps: Calcium produces a brick-red flame colour.
This helps identify it in simple qualitative analysis.
It should not be confused with sodium’s yellow flame.
Therefore the characteristic flame colour is brick-red.
explanation.simple_explanation: Calcium gives a brick-red flame. Check our "Tutorial section" for flame tests.

subtopic: Alkaline earth metals, especially calcium: lime cycle
question: Which sequence correctly shows the lime cycle up to slaked lime?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct chemical sequence in the lime cycle.
explanation.principle: Limestone is first heated, and the quicklime formed is then slaked with water.
steps: Calcium carbonate is heated to form calcium oxide.
Calcium oxide is quicklime.
Adding water to quicklime gives calcium hydroxide.
Therefore the correct sequence is CaCO₃ → CaO → Ca(OH)₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: The lime cycle is very useful in revision of calcium compounds. Check our "Tutorial section" for the full cycle.

question: The principal ore of aluminium is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the principal ore of aluminium.
explanation.principle: Bauxite is the main natural source used for aluminium production.
steps: It contains hydrated aluminium oxides together with impurities.
It is purified to obtain alumina.
This makes it the chief ore of aluminium.
Therefore the principal ore is bauxite.
explanation.simple_explanation: Bauxite is the main ore used in aluminium extraction. Check our "Tutorial section" for ore names.

question: The main product obtained after purifying bauxite is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the product of bauxite purification.
explanation.principle: The purification step provides aluminium oxide for electrolytic extraction.
steps: Unwanted substances are removed from the ore.
The useful oxide obtained is alumina, Al₂O₃.
This is the feed material for extraction.
Therefore the main product is alumina.
explanation.simple_explanation: Purified alumina is used for aluminium extraction. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: Aluminium is extracted from purified alumina by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the extraction method of aluminium.
explanation.principle: Aluminium is too reactive to be extracted by simple carbon reduction.
steps: Al₂O₃ is a very stable oxide.
Electrolysis is therefore used to release aluminium metal.
This is the standard industrial method.
Therefore aluminium is extracted by electrolysis.
explanation.simple_explanation: Electrolysis is the usual extraction method for very reactive metals. Check our "Tutorial section" for extraction methods.

question: Why is aluminium not usually extracted by heating its oxide with carbon?

options.B: carbon is not a reducing agent

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why carbon reduction is not the usual extraction method for aluminium.
explanation.principle: The stability of aluminium oxide makes ordinary carbon reduction unsuitable.
steps: Aluminium is high in the reactivity series.
Its oxide, Al₂O₃, is very stable.
Carbon does not reduce it in the normal extraction route used for less reactive metals.
Therefore aluminium is not usually extracted by carbon reduction because its oxide is too stable.
explanation.final_answer: A. its oxide is too stable
explanation.simple_explanation: Reactivity and oxide stability explain extraction choices. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: Aluminium is useful for aircraft bodies mainly because it is

options.B: light and fairly strong when alloyed

answer_text: light and fairly strong when alloyed

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify why aluminium is useful in aircraft construction.
explanation.principle: Aircraft materials should be light but strong.
steps: Aluminium has relatively low density.
When made into suitable alloys, it gains useful strength.
This makes it valuable where low mass is important.
Therefore aluminium is useful mainly because it is light and fairly strong when alloyed.
explanation.final_answer: B. light and fairly strong when alloyed
explanation.simple_explanation: Aluminium’s low density is very important in transport uses. Check our "Tutorial section" for applications.

question: Aluminium resists corrosion in air because it quickly forms

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why aluminium is resistant to corrosion.
explanation.principle: The surface oxide layer protects the metal beneath.
steps: Fresh aluminium reacts with oxygen in air.
A thin layer of aluminium oxide forms.
This layer adheres firmly and reduces further attack.
Therefore aluminium resists corrosion because it forms a protective oxide film.
explanation.simple_explanation: The oxide film is the key to aluminium’s corrosion resistance. Check our "Tutorial section" for corrosion protection.

question: A domestic use of aluminium is linked to its ability to

explanation.question_restate: We are to connect a property of aluminium with a household use.
explanation.principle: Cookware and related items need good heat conduction.
steps: Aluminium transfers heat efficiently.
This makes it suitable for cooking utensils.
Its low density also makes such utensils easier to handle.
Therefore the domestic use is linked to its ability to conduct heat well.
explanation.simple_explanation: Heat conduction helps explain many kitchen uses of aluminium. Check our "Tutorial section" for property–use links.

question: When NaOH is added in small amount to Al³⁺ solution, the precipitate formed is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the precipitate formed by Al³⁺ with hydroxide ions.
explanation.principle: Aluminium ions react with OH⁻ to produce aluminium hydroxide.
steps: A small amount of NaOH provides hydroxide ions.
These combine with Al³⁺ to form Al(OH)₃.
This precipitate is white and gelatinous.
Therefore the precipitate is Al(OH)₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Aluminium hydroxide is an important precipitate in ion tests. Check our "Tutorial section" for qualitative analysis.

question: A white precipitate formed from Al³⁺ with NaOH dissolves in excess NaOH because aluminium hydroxide is

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why aluminium hydroxide dissolves in excess sodium hydroxide.
explanation.principle: Amphoteric hydroxides react with both acids and strong bases.
steps: Al(OH)₃ first appears as a white precipitate.
In excess NaOH, it reacts further and dissolves.
This is characteristic of amphoteric behavior.
Therefore it dissolves because aluminium hydroxide is amphoteric.
explanation.simple_explanation: Amphoterism is a key idea in aluminium ion tests. Check our "Tutorial section" for amphoteric hydroxides.

question: The purified oxide from which aluminium is obtained is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the purified oxide used in aluminium extraction.
explanation.principle: This purified oxide is called alumina.
steps: Bauxite is purified first.
The substance obtained is aluminium oxide, Al₂O₃.
It is then used in electrolytic extraction.
Therefore the purified oxide is Al₂O₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Alumina is the purified oxide used for aluminium production. Check our "Tutorial section" for extraction steps.

question: Cassiterite is mainly composed of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the composition of cassiterite.
explanation.principle: Cassiterite is tin(IV) oxide.
steps: It is the chief ore of tin.
Tin is in the +4 state in this ore.
The formula is SnO₂.
Therefore cassiterite is mainly composed of SnO₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Cassiterite is tin(IV) oxide. Check our "Tutorial section" for ore formulas.

question: Tin can be extracted from cassiterite because cassiterite can be

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why tin can be obtained from cassiterite by furnace methods.
explanation.principle: Tin is not too high in the reactivity series for carbon reduction.
steps: Cassiterite is SnO₂.
Carbon can remove oxygen from this oxide under suitable conditions.
That leaves metallic tin.
Therefore tin can be extracted because cassiterite can be reduced by carbon.
explanation.simple_explanation: Carbon reduction works for metals lower in the reactivity series. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: Tin is often used in plating food cans because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify why tin is suitable for coating food cans.
explanation.principle: A coating metal should not corrode easily.
steps: Tin is more resistant to corrosion than iron.
When it coats steel, it helps protect the steel beneath.
This reduces rusting and preserves the container.
Therefore tin is used because it resists corrosion.
explanation.simple_explanation: Tin-plating helps protect steel against corrosion. Check our "Tutorial section" for uses of metals.

question: Tin is important in soft solder because it forms

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the usefulness of tin in solder.
explanation.principle: Solder needs to melt easily to join metal parts without very high temperatures.
steps: Tin mixes with lead and some other metals to form solder alloys.
These have relatively low melting points.
That makes them useful for joining wires and metal surfaces.
Therefore tin is important because it forms low-melting alloys.
explanation.simple_explanation: Solder is useful because it melts readily. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloy uses.

question: Which ore–metal pair is correctly matched?

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the correctly matched ore and metal.
explanation.principle: Cassiterite is the principal ore of tin.
steps: Bauxite is aluminium ore.
Haematite is iron ore.
Chalcopyrite is copper ore.
Therefore the correct match is cassiterite — tin.
explanation.simple_explanation: Matching ores to metals is common in objective questions. Check our "Tutorial section" for ore names.

question: Why can tin be extracted by carbon reduction while aluminium usually cannot?

options.A: tin is lower than aluminium in the reactivity series
options.C: carbon is not a reducing agent for tin

answer_text: tin is lower than aluminium in the reactivity series

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare extraction methods of tin and aluminium.
explanation.principle: The reactivity series helps predict extraction routes.
steps: Tin is less reactive than aluminium.
Its oxide can be reduced by carbon under furnace conditions.
Aluminium oxide is too stable for that ordinary method.
Therefore tin can be extracted by carbon reduction because it is lower than aluminium in the reactivity series.
explanation.final_answer: A. tin is lower than aluminium in the reactivity series
explanation.simple_explanation: Reactivity explains why extraction methods differ. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: A common practical use of tin is in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify one practical use of tin.
explanation.principle: Tin is often used as a coating metal.
steps: Steel can rust easily in moist air.
A coating of tin helps protect the steel surface.
This is seen in many food cans and containers.
Therefore a common use of tin is protective coating of steel.
explanation.simple_explanation: Tin-plating is one of the best-known uses of tin. Check our "Tutorial section" for corrosion control.

question: Which statement about tin is correct?

options.A: it is commonly obtained from cassiterite
options.B: it is the chief component of limestone

answer_text: it is commonly obtained from cassiterite

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a correct statement about tin.
explanation.principle: Cassiterite is the standard ore associated with tin.
steps: Tin is a metal, not a non-metal.
It forms useful alloys and protective coatings.
Its chief ore is cassiterite.
Therefore the correct statement is that it is commonly obtained from cassiterite.
explanation.final_answer: A. it is commonly obtained from cassiterite
explanation.simple_explanation: Ore names are important in extraction questions. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: The oxide ore of tin is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the oxide ore of tin.
explanation.principle: The main oxide ore of tin is cassiterite.
steps: Cassiterite has the formula SnO₂.
This is tin(IV) oxide.
It is the ore from which tin is extracted.
Therefore the oxide ore of tin is SnO₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Cassiterite is tin(IV) oxide. Check our "Tutorial section" for ore formulas.

question: The main purpose of tin-plating is to

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the purpose of coating steel with tin.
explanation.principle: Protective metal coatings are used to slow corrosion.
steps: Iron and steel rust in the presence of air and water.
A tin coating shields the steel from direct exposure.
This reduces rusting.
Therefore the main purpose of tin-plating is to reduce rusting of steel.
explanation.simple_explanation: Coatings are a practical method of corrosion control. Check our "Tutorial section" for applied metal chemistry.

question: Many first transition series elements are grouped together because they involve the filling of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the orbitals associated with the first transition series.
explanation.principle: The d-subshell is central to the chemistry of these elements.
steps: As we move across the first transition series, d-orbitals become occupied.
This helps explain their shared properties.
These include coloured ions and variable oxidation states.
Therefore the first transition series involves the filling of d-orbitals.
explanation.simple_explanation: The d-subshell is the basis of transition-metal chemistry. Check our "Tutorial section" for d-block revision.

question: Variable oxidation states in transition metals are possible mainly because

options.A: their s and d electrons are close in energy

answer_text: their s and d electrons are close in energy

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why many transition metals show several oxidation states.
explanation.principle: The available electrons used in bonding come from orbitals of similar energy.
steps: The s and d electrons of transition metals are not very different in energy.
Different numbers of these electrons may be used in forming compounds.
This gives rise to several oxidation states.
Therefore variable oxidation states are possible mainly because their s and d electrons are close in energy.
explanation.final_answer: A. their s and d electrons are close in energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Close s- and d-electron energies help explain variable oxidation states. Check our "Tutorial section" for transition metals.

question: A common property of many transition-metal ions in solution is that they are

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common observable property of many transition-metal ions.
explanation.principle: Colour is a famous feature of many transition-metal ions.
steps: Solutions of many transition-metal ions show characteristic colours.
This arises from d-electron transitions.
These colours are useful in qualitative analysis.
Therefore many transition-metal ions in solution are coloured.
explanation.simple_explanation: Transition-metal ions are often recognized by their colours. Check our "Tutorial section" for examples.

question: A transition-metal ion forms a complex when it bonds to ligands through

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the type of bonding involved in complex-ion formation.
explanation.principle: Ligands form coordinate bonds to the central metal ion.
steps: Ligands donate lone pairs of electrons.
The central metal ion accepts these electron pairs.
This creates coordinate covalent bonds.
Therefore a complex forms through electron-pair donation.
explanation.simple_explanation: Coordinate bonding is central to complex-ion chemistry. Check our "Tutorial section" for ligands and complexes.

question: A transition metal used as a catalyst is useful because a catalyst

options.A: speeds a reaction without being used up overall
options.C: always changes the equilibrium position permanently

answer_text: speeds a reaction without being used up overall

explanation.question_restate: We are to define a catalyst in a context involving transition metals.
explanation.principle: A catalyst provides a faster route for the reaction.
steps: The catalyst lowers the effective activation energy.
It is regenerated by the overall end of the mechanism.
That means it is not used up overall.
Therefore a catalyst speeds a reaction without being used up overall.
explanation.final_answer: A. speeds a reaction without being used up overall
explanation.simple_explanation: Catalysts increase rate without being consumed overall. Check our "Tutorial section" for catalysis basics.

subtopic: First transition series: comparison with main-group metals
question: Many sodium salts are colourless while many transition-metal salts are coloured because transition-metal ions often have

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare sodium salts with transition-metal salts.
explanation.principle: The partly filled d-subshell of transition-metal ions allows visible-light absorption.
steps: Sodium ions do not have partly filled d-orbitals.
Many transition-metal ions do.
This difference leads to d-electron transitions and visible colour in many transition-metal salts.
Therefore transition-metal salts are often coloured because the ions have partly filled d-subshells.
explanation.simple_explanation: Partly filled d-subshells explain many colours in transition-metal salts. Check our "Tutorial section" for coloured ions.

question: The fact that iron forms both Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ means that iron shows

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the concept shown by the existence of Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺.
explanation.principle: A metal with more than one common oxidation state shows variable oxidation states.
steps: Fe²⁺ is iron in the +2 oxidation state.
Fe³⁺ is iron in the +3 oxidation state.
This is a classic example from transition-metal chemistry.
Therefore iron shows variable oxidation states.
explanation.simple_explanation: Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ are standard examples of variable oxidation states. Check our "Tutorial section" for examples.

question: A ligand is best described as a species that

options.A: donates a lone pair to a metal ion

answer_text: donates a lone pair to a metal ion

explanation.question_restate: We are to define a ligand.
explanation.principle: Ligands are species involved in coordinate bonding to metal ions.
steps: A ligand may be neutral or negatively charged.
Its important feature is possession of a lone pair.
That electron pair is donated to the metal ion.
Therefore a ligand is a species that donates a lone pair to a metal ion.
explanation.final_answer: A. donates a lone pair to a metal ion
explanation.simple_explanation: Ligands are central to the formation of metal complexes. Check our "Tutorial section" for coordination chemistry.

question: One reason transition metals make effective catalysts is that they can

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain another reason transition metals act as catalysts.
explanation.principle: Surface catalysis often depends on adsorption of reacting particles.
steps: Reactant molecules can be held on the metal surface.
This weakens bonds and makes reaction easier.
Together with variable oxidation states, this enhances catalytic action.
Therefore one reason transition metals make effective catalysts is that they can adsorb reactants on their surfaces.
explanation.final_answer: A. adsorb reactants on their surfaces
explanation.simple_explanation: Adsorption is important in many catalytic processes. Check our "Tutorial section" for catalysis.

question: Which of the following is a typical property of many transition metals?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a typical transition-metal property.
explanation.principle: Transition metals often differ from main-group metals because of their d-electrons.
steps: Many transition-metal compounds are coloured.
They often show variable oxidation states and catalytic activity as well.
This makes coloured compounds a typical property.
Therefore the correct answer is formation of coloured compounds.
explanation.simple_explanation: Coloured compounds are a classic hallmark of transition-metal chemistry. Check our "Tutorial section" for d-block revision.

question: An important iron ore is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an important ore of iron.
explanation.principle: Haematite is one of the major ores used in iron extraction.
steps: Haematite contains iron(III) oxide.
It is widely used in blast furnace extraction.
This makes it one of the standard ore names to know.
Therefore the important iron ore is haematite.
explanation.simple_explanation: Haematite is a major ore of iron. Check our "Tutorial section" for ore names.

question: In the blast furnace, limestone is added mainly to

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the function of limestone in the blast furnace.
explanation.principle: A flux helps remove impurities from the ore.
steps: Limestone decomposes to give calcium oxide.
This calcium oxide reacts with silica impurities.
The product forms slag, which can be removed.
Therefore limestone is added mainly to act as a flux.
explanation.simple_explanation: Fluxes help remove impurities during extraction. Check our "Tutorial section" for blast furnace chemistry.

question: The main purpose of carbon(II) oxide in the blast furnace is to

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the role of carbon(II) oxide in iron extraction.
explanation.principle: CO acts as the main reducing agent in the blast furnace.
steps: Iron ore contains oxygen combined with iron.
CO removes this oxygen.
This leaves iron metal and forms CO₂.
Therefore its main purpose is to reduce iron ore.
explanation.simple_explanation: CO is the main reducing agent in iron extraction. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: Iron is widely used in structural work mainly because it can be converted into

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why iron is so important in structural work.
explanation.principle: The usefulness of iron is increased greatly when it is alloyed to form steel.
steps: Steel has improved strength and mechanical properties compared with pure iron.
This makes it ideal for bridges, buildings and machines.
Iron is therefore valuable because it can be converted into steel.
Therefore the correct answer is steel.
explanation.simple_explanation: The industrial importance of iron is closely linked to steel production. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloys.

question: The ion Fe²⁺ is commonly called

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the correct name for Fe²⁺.
explanation.principle: The Roman numeral shows the oxidation state of the metal ion.
steps: Fe²⁺ means iron has a +2 charge.
The correct name is iron(II) ion.
Fe³⁺ would be iron(III) ion.
Therefore Fe²⁺ is called the iron(II) ion.
explanation.simple_explanation: Use the charge to choose the correct oxidation-state name. Check our "Tutorial section" for ion naming.

question: The precipitate formed when Fe³⁺ reacts with NaOH is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the hydroxide formed by Fe³⁺ with NaOH.
explanation.principle: Hydroxide ions combine with Fe³⁺ to produce iron(III) hydroxide.
steps: The iron(III) ion has a +3 charge.
Three hydroxide ions are needed to balance this.
The product is Fe(OH)₃, which is brown.
Therefore the precipitate formed is Fe(OH)₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: Writing the correct hydroxide formula is important in qualitative analysis. Check our "Tutorial section" for ion tests.

question: The brown precipitate obtained with Fe³⁺ and NaOH is due to formation of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the substance responsible for the brown precipitate from Fe³⁺ with NaOH.
explanation.principle: This is the standard hydroxide test for iron(III).
steps: Hydroxide ions react with Fe³⁺.
The product formed is Fe(OH)₃.
This appears as a brown precipitate.
Therefore the brown precipitate is iron(III) hydroxide.
explanation.simple_explanation: Fe³⁺ is commonly identified by a brown hydroxide precipitate. Check our "Tutorial section" for qualitative analysis.

question: Compared with pure iron, steel is usually

options.B: stronger and more useful in engineering

answer_text: stronger and more useful in engineering

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare steel with pure iron.
explanation.principle: Alloying iron improves many of its practical properties.
steps: Steel contains iron plus carbon and sometimes other elements.
This often increases strength and hardness.
That is why steel is more useful in many engineering jobs.
Therefore steel is usually stronger and more useful in engineering.
explanation.final_answer: B. stronger and more useful in engineering
explanation.simple_explanation: Steel is widely used because alloying improves the properties of iron. Check our "Tutorial section" for alloys.

question: The slag formed in the blast furnace helps to remove

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify what slag removes in the blast furnace.
explanation.principle: Slag formation is part of impurity removal during extraction.
steps: Calcium oxide from limestone reacts with silica impurity.
This forms calcium silicate slag.
The slag can be separated from the molten iron.
Therefore slag helps to remove silica impurity.
explanation.simple_explanation: Slag removes impurities from the furnace mixture. Check our "Tutorial section" for blast furnace chemistry.

question: One major reason iron remains industrially important is that it

options.A: forms useful alloys such as steel

answer_text: forms useful alloys such as steel

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the industrial importance of iron.
explanation.principle: Iron is made much more useful through alloy formation.
steps: Pure iron has limited uses compared with steel and related alloys.
Iron-based alloys have improved mechanical properties.
This gives iron a central place in engineering and construction.
Therefore iron remains important because it forms useful alloys such as steel.
explanation.final_answer: A. forms useful alloys such as steel
explanation.simple_explanation: Alloy formation greatly increases the usefulness of iron. Check our "Tutorial section" for materials chemistry.

question: A common sulphide ore of copper is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common sulphide ore of copper.
explanation.principle: Chalcopyrite is one of the chief ores of copper.
steps: It is important in copper extraction.
This makes it a standard ore name to remember.
It is different from haematite, bauxite and cassiterite.
Therefore the common sulphide ore is chalcopyrite.
explanation.simple_explanation: Chalcopyrite is a common sulphide ore of copper. Check our "Tutorial section" for ore names.

question: Copper can be obtained from copper(II) oxide because copper is

options.A: low enough in the reactivity series to allow reduction

answer_text: low enough in the reactivity series to allow reduction

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why copper can be obtained by reduction from its oxide.
explanation.principle: Less reactive metals are often extracted by chemical reduction.
steps: Copper is below very reactive metals like sodium and aluminium.
Its oxide can be reduced by carbon or carbon(II) oxide.
That allows extraction without electrolysis.

explanation.final_answer: A. low enough in the reactivity series to allow reduction
explanation.simple_explanation: Reactivity helps determine extraction methods. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

question: A major reason copper is used in electrical wiring is that it

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main property behind the use of copper in wiring.
explanation.principle: Good electrical wires require very good conductivity.
steps: Copper allows current to flow easily.
It is also ductile enough to be drawn into wires.
These features make it extremely useful in electrical work.
Therefore copper is used because it conducts electricity well.
explanation.simple_explanation: Copper is a leading wiring metal because of its conductivity. Check our "Tutorial section" for property–use links.

question: Copper is also useful in cookware because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain a second important use of copper.
explanation.principle: Cookware should transfer heat effectively.
steps: Copper has very good thermal conductivity.
This helps heat spread evenly across the utensil.
That is why copper is valuable in some cookware.
Therefore copper is useful because it conducts heat efficiently.
explanation.simple_explanation: Copper’s heat conduction helps explain its use in cooking utensils. Check our "Tutorial section" for metals and their uses.

question: The formula CuSO₄ represents

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the name corresponding to the formula CuSO₄.

SO₄²⁻ is the tetraoxosulphate(VI) ion.

explanation.simple_explanation: Name formulas carefully by identifying both the cation and anion. Check our "Tutorial section" for naming practice.

question: When hydrated CuSO₄ crystals are heated strongly, they

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain what happens to hydrated copper(II) sulphate on strong heating.

explanation.simple_explanation: Heating hydrated salts often removes water of crystallization. Check our "Tutorial section" for hydrated compounds.

question: The blue precipitate formed when NaOH is added to Cu²⁺ solution is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the blue precipitate formed by Cu²⁺ and hydroxide ions.

This hydroxide appears as a blue precipitate.

explanation.simple_explanation: Cu²⁺ gives a blue hydroxide precipitate with NaOH. Check our "Tutorial section" for qualitative analysis.

question: The deep blue solution formed when excess NH₃ is added to Cu²⁺ is due to

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the deep blue solution seen with copper(II) ions and excess ammonia.

question: Copper is often preferred to iron in electric cables because copper

explanation.final_answer: A. conducts better and resists corrosion better
explanation.simple_explanation: Material choice usually depends on more than one property. Check our "Tutorial section" for comparisons.

question: Copper is important industrially not only on its own but also because it forms alloys such as

question: Ordinary steel is mainly composed of
